finding.title: "Deal damage equal to your [armor]" is actually armor × STR-mult + Rage − enemy defense, not 1:1
finding.detail: Shield Bash's only effect is {type:'damage', value:0, target:'enemy', scale:{stat:'vit', per:1, value:1, source:'armor'}}. In CardResolver.applyEffect the armor-source path sets resolvedValue = heroDefense, but the damage then goes through the FULL damage pipeline: baseDmg = (resolvedValue + hitBonus + rageBonus) * strMult * ... and, because pierce_armor is NOT set, enemy defense is subtracted (perHit = max(1, floor(baseDmg - enemyDef))). So the hit is amplified by STR (strMult = 1+(STR-1)*0.25), increased by banked Rage (up to +12), and reduced by the enemy's armor. It only equals your armor at STR=1, 0 Rage, 0 enemy defense. Simulated: armor 20 / STR 4 → 35 dmg; armor 20 / STR 1 vs enemyDef 8 → 12 dmg. The phrase 'equal to your [armor]' tells the player none of this.
finding.evidence: Effect: {"type":"damage","value":0,"target":"enemy","scale":{"stat":"vit","per":1,"value":1,"source":"armor"}}. Resolver: resolvedValue=heroDefense (line 369-373); baseDmg=(resolvedValue+hitBonus+rageBonus)*strMult*... (line 434); non-pierce → Math.max(1, Math.floor(baseDmg - effectiveEnemyDef)) (line 442). Sim: armor=20,STR=4 → 35; armor=20,STR=1,enemyDef=8 → 12.
finding.suggested_fix: Either (a) make the wording reflect reality (e.g. "Deal damage based on your [armor]" and let the standard STR/defense conventions apply, like Granite Lunge's honest "Deal 4, +1 per 4[armor]"), or (b) if a literal 1:1 is intended, set pierce_armor and bypass STR/rage scaling for this effect so the number truly equals current armor.

verdict.title: "Deal damage equal to your [armor]" is actually armor × STR-mult + Rage − enemy defense, not 1:1
verdict.reason: Confirmed by direct code inspection. The card's only effect is {type:'damage', value:0, target:'enemy', scale:{stat:'vit', per:1, value:1, source:'armor'}} (cards.json line 950-961), and the stored description is "Deal damage equal to your [armor]." In CardResolver.applyEffect, the armor-source path (lines 368-373) computes resolvedValue = 0 + floor(heroDefense/1)*1 = heroDefense. But the value then flows through the FULL damage pipeline at line 434: baseDmg = (resolvedValue + hitBonus + rageBonus) * strMult * ... where strMult = 1 + (STR-1)*0.25 (line 416) and rageBonus = min(rageStacks, 12) (line 425). pierce_armor is NOT set on the effect (it would come from effect.pierce_armor at line 298, absent here), so pierceArmor defaults false and enemy defense is subtracted: perHit = max(1, floor(baseDmg - effectiveEnemyDef)) (line 442). I reproduced the finding's exact numbers: armor 20 / STR 4 → 35 damage; armor 20 / STR 1 / enemyDef 8 → 12 damage; it only equals armor (20) at STR 1, 0 Rage, 0 enemy def. The description tells the player none of the STR amplification, Rage bonus, or enemy-armor subtraction. This is not an intended C1-C5 design choice — STR-as-multiplier and Rage-as-flat-bonus are intended engine conventions, but the CARD'S TEXT contradicts what those conventions produce (a non-1:1 number), so it violates its own stated wording. Note: scale.stat is 'vit' but source:'armor' overrides it to read heroDefense (line 370), so the 'vit' label is inert/misleading too, though harmless to output.
verdict.corrected_detail: Shield Bash's effect {type:'damage', value:0, scale:{stat:'vit', per:1, value:1, source:'armor'}} resolves to resolvedValue=heroDefense, then is amplified by strMult=1+(STR-1)*0.25 and rageBonus (capped 12), and since pierce_armor is unset, enemy defense is subtracted. So "Deal damage equal to your [armor]" is literally accurate only at STR=1, 0 Rage, 0 enemy defense; in normal T2 play it deviates (e.g. armor 20/STR 4 = 35; armor 20/STR 1 vs enemyDef 8 = 12). Recommend either rewording to "Deal damage based on your [armor]" (matching the honest convention used by Granite Lunge) or, if a true 1:1 is intended, setting pierce_armor and bypassing STR/Rage scaling for this effect.

finding.title: Vengeance "+4 seconds" actually adds a SECOND bleed-on-hit aura (double bleed for 4s), not a duration extension
finding.detail: Razor Stance has two aura effects: a 10s on_hit_dealt bleed aura (unconditional) and a SECOND 4s on_hit_dealt bleed aura gated by condition took_damage_within_ms:2000. The formatter collapses these into "Vengeance: +4 seconds", implying the bleed-on-hit window lasts 14s total. But the engine does not extend the first aura — it pushes an independent second aura. fireRecurringTrigger iterates ALL matching auras with no dedup, so while BOTH auras are alive (the first 4 seconds, if Vengeance fired at cast) every hit applies bleed TWICE, then single bleed for the remaining 6s. Net behavior is 'double bleed for 4s', not '4 extra seconds of bleed' as the text reads.
finding.evidence: effects[0]: aura ttl 10000 on_hit_dealt → dot bleed. effects[1]: aura ttl 4000 on_hit_dealt → dot bleed, condition took_damage_within_ms:2000. StatusEffects.fireRecurringTrigger loops every aura with matching trigger (no dedup), so 2 auras fire on one hit. formatCardDescription output: "...apply 1[bleed]([dex]). Vengeance: +4 seconds."
finding.suggested_fix: Reword to reflect the real effect, e.g. "Vengeance: also apply 1 more [bleed] on each hit for 4 seconds" (or for the first 4s). If a literal duration extension is intended, change the engine to extend the existing aura's remainingMs instead of arming a second aura.

verdict.title: Vengeance "+4 seconds" actually arms a SECOND concurrent bleed-on-hit aura (double bleed per hit while overlapping), not a duration extension
verdict.reason: Confirmed by data + engine code. cards.json effects[] has TWO aura effects: effects[0] ttl_ms 10000, trigger on_hit_dealt, then→dot bleed (unconditional); effects[1] ttl_ms 4000, trigger on_hit_dealt, then→dot bleed, condition took_damage_within_ms:2000. These are independent auras, not a single extendable one. CardResolver.ts case 'aura' (lines 694-704) pushes each into state.heroAuras via createAura — no merge/extend of the first aura's remainingMs. On a hit, fireRecurringTrigger (StatusEffects.ts lines 303-310) loops over EVERY aura with trigger==='on_hit_dealt' and pushes its `then` payload, with NO dedup; per-aura refire is gated only by cooldownMs (line 309), and neither aura sets cooldown_ms, so both fire on every hit. Therefore while both auras are alive each hit applies bleed TWICE. The second aura is armed only if, at cast time, the condition gate passes — CardResolver lines 258-262 evaluate took_damage_within_ms at the top of effect resolution and `return` (skipping aura creation) if lastHeroDamageMs is null or the window elapsed. When armed it lives 4000ms, so the double-bleed window is the first ~4s, single bleed for the remaining ~6s. The formatter deliberately mislabels this: applyVengeanceDurationExtension (CardText.ts lines 1222-1230) matches the second aura's body against the first's identical body and rewrites "Vengeance: For 4 seconds, ..." into "Vengeance: +4 seconds" — its own comment says "Collapse a Vengeance aura that only extends the duration of an identical base aura," but the engine does not extend any duration. Stored description: "For 10 seconds, every time you hit an enemy, apply 1[bleed]([dex]). Vengeance: +4 seconds." reads as a 10s→14s extension of a single bleed-on-hit window; real behavior is "for 4s also apply a second bleed per hit (only if you took damage recently at cast)." Genuine desc_mismatch/unclear_desc; med severity is right (functional bleed engine, but text misstates magnitude and trigger).
verdict.corrected_detail: Razor Stance arms two independent on_hit_dealt bleed auras. effects[0] (ttl 10000, unconditional) and effects[1] (ttl 4000, condition took_damage_within_ms:2000). The Vengeance aura is created at cast only when the hero took HP damage within 2000ms (CardResolver lines 258-262 gate the effect and return otherwise; lastHeroDamageMs starts null). When created it is a SEPARATE aura — createAura at CardResolver line 702-704 pushes it; the first aura's remainingMs is never extended. fireRecurringTrigger (StatusEffects lines 303-310) iterates all matching auras with no dedup and no shared cooldown (neither aura sets cooldown_ms), so during the ~4s overlap every hit applies bleed TWICE, then single bleed for the remaining ~6s. The formatter's applyVengeanceDurationExtension (CardText lines 1222-1230) collapses the identical-body Vengeance aura into "Vengeance: +4 seconds," falsely implying a 10s→14s extension of a single bleed-on-hit window. Suggested fix: reword to reflect the real effect (e.g. "Vengeance: also apply 1 more [bleed] on each hit for 4 seconds") or, if a literal extension is intended, change the engine to bump the existing aura's remainingMs instead of arming a second aura.

finding.target: t1-water (Mend), and all T2 heal cards (Mist Step, Bloodtide Mend, Vow of the Tide, Misting Veil, Mire Bloom, Steam Surge)
finding.title: Healing has an undocumented global SPI multiplier on top of (or instead of) the shown ([spi]) scaler
finding.detail: CardResolver heal case applies spiBonus = floor(resolvedValue * (heroSpirit * 0.15)) AFTER resolvedValue already includes any explicit ([spi]) additive scaler. So heal cards that show ([spi]) double-dip on SPI, and Mend — which shows NO scaler ('Heal 9') — still scales heavily with SPI (Heal 9 at SPI=10 → 9 × 2.5 = 22). This parallels the intended hidden STR damage multiplier convention, so it may be intentional, but unlike STR it is not in the stated C1–C5 intent and the explicit ([spi]) tags understate SPI's true contribution, which can mislead deck-building decisions.
finding.evidence: CardResolver line 455: const spiBonus = Math.floor(resolvedValue * (state.heroSpirit * 0.15)); totalHeal = resolvedValue + spiBonus. t1-water effects: [{"type":"heal","value":9,"target":"self"}] (no scale) yet still SPI-multiplied. Mire Bloom 'Heal 3([spi])' gets both the +1/3-SPI add and the ×15%/pt multiplier.
finding.suggested_fix: Either document the global SPI heal multiplier the same way STR is treated as a known convention, or drop the redundant explicit ([spi]) scalers on heal effects so the per-card text isn't understating SPI twice over.

verdict.target: t1-water (Mend) + all T2 heal cards (Mist Step, Bloodtide Mend, Vow of the Tide, Misting Veil, Mire Bloom, Steam Surge)
verdict.title: Healing has an undocumented global SPI multiplier on top of the shown ([spi]) scaler
verdict.reason:
Confirmed in code. CardResolver.ts heal case (lines 451-456) applies a global SPI multiplier AFTER the explicit scale clause is already baked in. Line 368-373: `let resolvedValue = value; ... resolvedValue = value + Math.floor(statValue / scale.per) * scale.value` — so resolvedValue already contains the additive ([spi]) bonus. Then line 455-456: `const spiBonus = Math.floor(resolvedValue * (state.heroSpirit * 0.15)); const totalHeal = resolvedValue + spiBonus;`.

Evidence per card:
- Mend (t1-water, cards.json:161-167): effects = [{"type":"heal","value":9,"target":"self"}] with NO scale; description "Heal 9." Yet at SPI=10 it heals floor(9*(10*0.15))=13 bonus + 9 = 22, not 9. The text gives the player zero indication SPI matters at all.
- Mire Bloom (cards.json:1713,1731-1740): description "...Heal 3([spi])..." with scale {stat:"spi",per:3,value:1}. This double-dips: the ([spi]) tag is the +1-per-3-SPI additive (raises resolvedValue), and the same SPI then drives the hidden ×15%/pt global multiplier on the already-raised value.

CardText.ts healBody (lines 459-468) only renders the explicit scale clause as "Heal N([spi])"; nothing communicates the global multiplier. So the displayed text understates SPI's true contribution (Mend understates it entirely; ([spi])-tagged cards understate the magnitude).

This is a genuine unclear_desc: a player reading the card cannot determine how much SPI changes the heal. It is NOT covered by C1-C5: the stated intent documents STR (universal mult 1+(STR-1)*0.25) and magic elemMult (1+(stat-1)*0.15, direct dmg only) as known conventions, but says nothing about a global SPI heal multiplier — and the code comment itself describes it as a deliberate "heal-archetype buff pass," confirming it is real and intentional engine behavior rather than a resolver misread. Keeping severity low: the effect works as the engine intends; the issue is purely text clarity, not a bug.
verdict.corrected_detail: The heal resolver applies a global +15%-per-SPI-point multiplier to every heal (CardResolver.ts:455-456), layered on top of any explicit ([spi]) additive scale clause that is already folded into resolvedValue (CardResolver.ts:368-373). Consequences: (1) Mend, which shows only "Heal 9." with no scaler, still scales heavily with SPI — at SPI=10 it heals 22, not 9. (2) Cards that show "Heal N([spi])" (e.g. Mire Bloom's "Heal 3([spi])") double-dip on SPI: the visible tag is only the additive component, while the same stat also drives the hidden multiplicative component. CardText.healBody (CardText.ts:459-468) never surfaces the global multiplier, so per-card text understates SPI's real contribution. This is text-clarity only (the engine behaves as the "heal-archetype buff pass" comment intends), not a bug, and is not enumerated in C1-C5 the way the STR/elemMult conventions are. Fix: either document the global SPI heal multiplier as a known convention (like STR), or drop the redundant explicit ([spi]) scale clauses on heal effects so the text isn't understating SPI on two axes at once.

finding.title: Single-hit Quickstrike costs 2 stamina while comparable T2 attacks cost 1
finding.detail: Quickstrike is 'Deal 8([dex])' for cost {stamina:2}, cooldown 1.2. Nearly all other T2 attacks (Reckless Strike, Flurry Step, Sidestep & Slash, Tremor Dash, etc.) cost 1 stamina and deliver comparable or higher output with riders (bleed, armor, stamina refund). Paying double resource for a plain single hit with no secondary effect is a clear outlier on the primary power lever (resource cost). Flagging lightly per the sim-handled-elsewhere note; the description itself is accurate.
finding.evidence: t2-agility-attack: description 'Deal 8([dex]).', cost {"stamina":2}, cooldown 1.2, single effect damage. Peer T2 attacks (e.g. t2-agility-agility Flurry Step 'Deal 4. Deal 4([dex])' cost 1) deliver multi-effect value for half the resource.
finding.suggested_fix: Reduce cost to 1 stamina, or add a rider (bleed/stamina/haste) commensurate with the 2-stamina price, to bring it in line with the T2 attack curve.

verdict.title: Single-hit Quickstrike costs 2 stamina while comparable T2 attacks cost 1
verdict.reason: Confirmed as a low-severity balance_smell; the data is exactly as reported and the description is accurate (no desc/bug issue). cards.json t2-agility-attack: description "Deal 8([dex]).", single damage effect value 8 scale {dex, per 2, value 2}, cost {stamina:2}, cooldown 1.2. Per the resolver (CardResolver.ts applyEffect, line ~373: resolvedValue = value + Math.floor(statValue/per)*scale.value), Quickstrike yields 8 at DEX1, 12 at DEX4, 18 at DEX10 — a plain single hit with NO rider. Peer T2 attacks all cost 1 stamina AND carry secondary value: t2-agility-agility Flurry Step "Deal 4. Deal 4([dex])" (two hits, 8/9/11 at DEX1/4/10, cost 1) splits into two hits for per-hit/multi-hit interactions; t2-attack-attack Reckless Strike "Deal 9([str]). Gain 3[rage]. Apply 1[bleed] to yourself." (cost 1); t2-agility-counter Sidestep & Slash "Deal 5([dex]). Vengeance: deal 3 more. Apply 2[bleed]. Gain 1[stam]." (cost 1, refunds its own stamina); t2-agility-earth Tremor Dash "Deal 7([dex]). Gain 6[armor]([vit])." (cost 1, cd 1.6). Under C1 (resource cost is the PRIMARY power lever, cooldown stays snappy), paying double stamina for a rider-less single hit at the same 1.2 cd is a genuine outlier on that lever — not an intended-by-design choice that any text justifies. Correction to the original detail: at higher DEX Quickstrike's raw single-hit damage does eventually exceed Flurry Step's, so "comparable or higher output" only holds at low DEX; the robust issue is the cost-vs-rider mismatch, not raw damage. Keeping severity low because exact tuning is sim-handled elsewhere.
verdict.corrected_detail: Quickstrike (t2-agility-attack) is "Deal 8([dex])." (effects: single damage value 8, scale {stat:dex, per:2, value:2}) for cost {stamina:2}, cooldown 1.2 — a rider-less single hit. Resolver (CardResolver.ts ~L373) computes 8/12/18 damage at DEX 1/4/10. Comparable T2 attacks cost only 1 stamina and add secondary value: Flurry Step (t2-agility-agility, cost 1) splits into two hits (8/9/11 at DEX 1/4/10) enabling per-hit interactions; Reckless Strike (cost 1) adds 3 Rage + self-bleed; Sidestep & Slash (cost 1) adds vengeance damage, 2 bleed, and refunds 1 stamina; Tremor Dash (cost 1, cd 1.6) adds 6 armor. Under C1 (resource cost = primary power lever), the 2-stamina price for a no-rider single hit at a snappy 1.2 cd is the outlier. Note: at higher DEX Quickstrike's raw damage does surpass Flurry Step's, so the original "comparable or higher output" claim only holds at low DEX — the durable issue is cost vs rider, not raw output. Suggested fix: drop cost to 1 stamina, or add a rider (bleed/haste/stamina) befitting a 2-stamina price.

finding.title: Marsh Squall: self-applied poison inflates removal but not detonation damage (snapshot vs live mismatch)
finding.detail: Effects order is [dot apply 2 poison ([int]); damage 6 consume_stack_value:poison consume_fraction:0.5; stack -999 poison consume_stack consume_fraction:0.5]. CardResolver snapshots all stack pools ONCE at the start of the cast (preConsume = snapshotStacks(state), line 132), BEFORE the +2 poison is applied. The detonation damage uses ceil(snapshot*0.5)*6 — the PRE-CAST poison — so the freshly-applied poison never contributes to the burst. But the removal effect (case 'stack', consumeFrom) reads the LIVE pool (post-+2) and removes ceil(current*0.5). Net: the card pays damage on fewer stacks than it actually strips. The card is also EXHAUST (once per combat), so opening with it on a fresh target (0 poison) applies 2 poison, deals 0 burst, then strips 1 — a wasted exhaust. Compare to Drowning Lance (same partial-consume pattern, no self-apply) which is internally consistent.
finding.evidence: Resolver sim (INT1): 10 pre-poison -> dmg=30 (=6*ceil(10*0.5)=6*5), but after=6 (applied 2 ->12, removed ceil(12*0.5)=6). So 6 stacks removed, only 5 paid out as damage. INT7, 8 pre-poison -> dmg=24 (=6*ceil(8*0.5)=6*4) but applied 4 ([int] scale) ->12 and removed 6, leaving 6: 6 stacks stripped, only 4 counted for damage. 0 pre-poison -> dmg=0, after=1 (applied 2, removed 1). Description: "Consume half [poison]. Apply 2[poison]([int]). Deal 6 Pierce per [poison] consumed to all enemies."
finding.suggested_fix: Either (a) reorder so the +2 application precedes the snapshot used by the detonator (e.g. give the detonator a live/per_stack read so freshly-applied poison feeds the burst, matching its Pyre-detonator siblings), or (b) reword to make clear the detonation pays on the pre-cast pool only and the applied poison is for the lingering ramp, not this burst. The damage-count (ceil snapshot*frac) and removal-count (ceil current*frac) should reference the same pool.

verdict.title: Marsh Squall: self-applied poison inflates removal but not detonation damage (snapshot vs live mismatch)
verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED. Traced the exact code paths.

Effects order (cards.json L3602-3629): [1] dot apply 2 poison scale int per3 val1; [2] damage 6 pierce consume_stack_value:poison consume_fraction:0.5 target:aoe; [3] stack -999 poison consume_stack:true consume_fraction:0.5.

Snapshot timing: CardResolver.ts L132 `const preConsume = snapshotStacks(state)` is taken BEFORE the effects loop (L138-147), so the +2 poison from effect [1] is NOT in preConsume.

Damage path (detonator) reads the SNAPSHOT: L285 `const snap = preConsume[effect.consume_stack_value]` (pre-cast poison), L289-291 `consumedForDmg = ceil(snap * 0.5)`, L292 `effectiveValue *= consumedForDmg`. So burst = 6 * ceil(PRE-cast * 0.5).

Removal path reads the LIVE pool: L585-607, `consumeFrom(state.poisonStacks)` where the pool is post-(+2), L596 `take = ceil(cur * frac)`. So it strips ceil((pre-cast + 2 + intScale) * 0.5).

Verified numerics:
- INT1, 10 pre-poison: snap=10 -> dmg = 6*ceil(5)=30; pool 10+2=12, removed ceil(12*0.5)=6 -> after=6. 6 stripped, only 5 paid.
- INT7, 8 pre-poison: int scale floor(7/3)*1=2, applies 4 -> pool 12; dmg = 6*ceil(8*0.5)=24 (4 stacks); removed ceil(12*0.5)=6. 6 stripped, only 4 paid.
- 0 pre-poison: dmg=ceil(0)=0; applies 2 -> pool 2, removed 1 -> after=1. Card is exhaust:true (L171-174 marks spentThisCombat), so opening on a fresh target burns the exhaust for 0 burst.

Comparison sibling CONFIRMED: Drowning Lance (t3-attack-water-water, cards.json L5526-5548) has the identical partial-consume detonator (damage consume_stack_value:poison frac0.5 + stack -999 consume frac0.5) but NO self-apply before the snapshot, so snapshot==live and damage-count == removal-count. It is internally consistent; Marsh Squall is not because the +2 application sits between the snapshot and the removal.

Description mismatch: stored desc 'Consume half [poison]. Apply 2[poison]([int]). Deal 6 Pierce per [poison] consumed to all enemies.' CardText.consumeValuePhrase (L967-970) returns 'consumed' here (card truly consumes its own poison), so the text promises damage = 6 x (poison consumed). But the actual amount consumed (removal, on the live pool) exceeds the count the damage uses (the snapshot pool). A player reading 'per [poison] consumed' cannot tell the burst is computed on the pre-cast pool while the strip is on the post-application pool. This is a genuine card_bug/desc_mismatch, not C1-C5 intent.

Severity med is fair: a single T3 exhaust card whose burst undercounts vs its own strip and whose text is misleading; not game-breaking.
verdict.corrected_detail: Effects order is [dot apply 2 poison([int]); damage 6 Pierce consume_stack_value:poison consume_fraction:0.5 (aoe); stack -999 poison consume_stack consume_fraction:0.5]. CardResolver snapshots all stack pools ONCE before the effects loop (preConsume = snapshotStacks(state), L132), BEFORE effect[1] applies +2 poison. The detonator (effect[2]) computes damage = 6 * ceil(snapshot*0.5) off the PRE-CAST poison (L285-292), so the freshly-applied +2 (and its [int] scale) never feed the burst. The removal (effect[3], case 'stack' consumeFrom) reads the LIVE pool (post-+2) and strips ceil(current*0.5) (L596). Net: the card strips more poison than it pays burst for. Verified: INT1 10 pre-poison -> dmg 30 (5 stacks) but strips ceil(12*0.5)=6; INT7 8 pre-poison -> dmg 24 (4 stacks) but strips 6. On a fresh target (0 poison) it deals 0 burst, strips 1, and burns its exhaust (exhaust:true, L171-174). Text 'Deal 6 Pierce per [poison] consumed' (consumeValuePhrase yields 'consumed') implies damage-count == consumed-count, but they reference different pools. Drowning Lance (t3-attack-water-water) has the same partial-consume pattern with no self-apply and is internally consistent — fix Marsh Squall by either reordering the +2 before the snapshot read (or giving the detonator a live read) or rewording so the burst is clearly on the pre-cast pool.

finding.target: consume_stack_value detonators vs Pyre-keyword detonators (Supernova/Marsh Squall/Drowning Lance/Tremor vs Cinderlance/Cinder Sprint/Venom Detonation)
finding.title: Two 'Consume all X and deal N per X consumed' implementations disagree on whether self-applied stacks count
finding.detail: Cards that apply a stack and then detonate it use one of two engine paths with identical-sounding prose but different counting. (1) Pyre-keyword path (condition.enemy_has_stack:X + per_stack) reads the LIVE pool when the damage effect resolves, so a stack the card applied earlier in the same cast IS counted. (2) consume_stack_value path reads the pre-cast snapshot (preConsume), so a stack applied earlier in the same cast is NOT counted. Cinder Sprint (Pyre) self-fuels its burst; Marsh Squall (consume_stack_value) does not — yet both read as 'consume ... and deal N per [X] consumed'. This makes the same wording mean two different things across the detonator set.
finding.evidence: Sim — Cinder Sprint (applies 3 burn, Pyre detonate value4): 0 pre-burn -> dmg=12 (=4*3, counts the 3 it applied); 5 pre-burn -> dmg=32 (=4*8, counts applied+pre). Venom Detonation (applies 2 burn, Pyre value2): 0 pre-burn -> dmg=4 (counts the 2 it applied). Marsh Squall (applies 2 poison, consume_stack_value): 0 pre-poison -> dmg=0 (does NOT count the 2 it applied).
finding.suggested_fix: Pick one counting rule for 'per [X] consumed' detonators and apply it consistently, or differentiate the prose (e.g. consume_stack_value detonators that pre-apply should say 'per [X] the enemy already had' / not promise the fresh application feeds the burst).

verdict.target: consume_stack_value detonators vs Pyre-keyword detonators (Supernova/Marsh Squall vs Cinder Sprint/Venom Detonation)
verdict.title: Two 'consume X and deal N per X consumed' implementations disagree on whether self-applied stacks count
verdict.reason:
Confirmed by reading CardResolver.ts. Two distinct engine paths back the identical-sounding prose 'deal N per [X] consumed', and they read the stack count at different times:

1) Pyre-keyword path (condition.enemy_has_stack + per_stack). CardResolver.ts:207-214 calls readStackCount(state, cond.enemy_has_stack, 'enemy') and multiplies effectiveValue by the LIVE pool at the instant the damage effect resolves. Because effects resolve in array order and dot/stack effects mutate state.burnStacks immediately (line 545: 'case burn: state.burnStacks += appliedValue'), any burn applied EARLIER in the same effects[] is included.
   - Cinder Sprint (t3-agility-fire-fire): effects[0] is dot value:3 stack:burn, effects[1] is damage with condition {enemy_has_stack:'burn', per_stack:true}. The self-applied 3 burn is in state.burnStacks when effects[1] reads it, so it IS counted. Desc: 'Apply 3[burn]. Consume all [burn] and deal 4([dex]) damage per [burn] consumed.'
   - Venom Detonation (t3-counter-fire-water): same shape — dot burn first, then Pyre damage; self-applied burn counted. Desc: 'Apply 2[burn]. ... Consume all [burn] and deal 2([str]) damage per [burn] consumed.'

2) consume_stack_value path. CardResolver.ts:132 takes preConsume = snapshotStacks(state) ONCE at cast start, BEFORE any effect resolves. Line 285 reads snap = preConsume[effect.consume_stack_value] and line 292 does effectiveValue *= consumedForDmg. So a stack applied earlier in the same cast is NOT in the snapshot and is NOT counted.
   - Marsh Squall (t3-air-earth-water): effects[0] is dot value:2 stack:poison, effects[1] is damage with consume_stack_value:'poison', consume_fraction:0.5. The damage reads the pre-cast poison snapshot (excludes the freshly applied 2), then * ceil(snap*0.5). With 0 pre-existing poison, dmg = 6 * ceil(0*0.5) = 0. Desc: 'Consume half [poison]. Apply 2[poison]([int]). Deal 6 Pierce per [poison] consumed to all enemies.'

So the SAME wording ('deal N per [X] consumed') means two different things: Cinder Sprint/Venom Detonation self-fuel the burst from the stack they just applied, while Marsh Squall does not. This is a genuine cross-card prose/behavior inconsistency, not intended-by-C1-C5 (no design directive distinguishes these; it is an artifact of which engine path each card happens to use).

Note: Supernova (consume_stack_value, no pre-applied burn) is NOT affected — it only consumes pre-existing burn, so its 'per [burn] consumed' wording is accurate. The defect specifically bites consume_stack_value detonators that ALSO pre-apply the same stack (Marsh Squall is the clear case). Severity kept low: it is a description/consistency issue, not a crash, and the consume_stack_value detonators still function (they just exclude the self-applied stack). The fix is to either unify the counting rule or reword the consume_stack_value+pre-apply cards (e.g. Marsh Squall) so the prose does not promise the fresh application feeds the burst.
verdict.corrected_detail: The two 'deal N per [X] consumed' detonator implementations count differently because they read the stack pool at different times. The Pyre-keyword path (condition.enemy_has_stack + per_stack) reads the LIVE pool when the damage resolves (CardResolver.ts:208,213), so a stack applied earlier in the same cast IS counted (Cinder Sprint self-fuels its 3 burn; Venom Detonation self-fuels its 2 burn). The consume_stack_value path reads a pre-cast snapshot taken once at cast start (CardResolver.ts:132,285,292), so a stack applied earlier in the same cast is NOT counted (Marsh Squall applies 2 poison that does not feed its own burst; with 0 pre-existing poison and consume_fraction 0.5 the burst deals 0). Same prose, two meanings. Supernova is unaffected because it does not pre-apply burn.

finding.title: Steaming Plague 'If enemy has [poison]' bonus always fires (self-satisfied gate)
finding.detail: Effects: [dot 3 poison aoe ([int]); dot 2 poison aoe ([int]) condition enemy_has_stack:poison]. The first effect unconditionally applies 3 poison, and the gate on the second effect reads the LIVE poison pool (readStackCount, lines 207-214), which is already >=3 by the time it evaluates. The condition therefore can never be false on a played card — the card always applies 5 poison. The conditional clause is purely cosmetic and misleads the player into thinking there is a precondition.
finding.evidence: Sim, 0 pre-poison INT1: after=5 poison (3 + 2). Description: "Apply 3[poison]([int]) to all enemies. If enemy has [poison]: apply 2 more [poison]([int])."
finding.suggested_fix: Drop the condition and fold into a flat 5 (or 3 + 2) application, or change the gate to a higher enemy_stack_atleast threshold so the bonus is genuinely conditional on PRE-EXISTING poison (like Slipvenom Tempo's poison>=10 gate).

verdict.title: Steaming Plague 'If enemy has [poison]' bonus always fires (self-satisfied gate)
verdict.reason: Confirmed by reading cards.json + CardResolver.ts. cards.json line 3479-3504: effect[0] is `dot value:3 stack:poison target:aoe` (unconditional, scales with int per3); effect[1] is `dot value:2 stack:poison` gated by `condition.enemy_has_stack:"poison"`. CardResolver applies effects sequentially in one cast (loop at line 139: `for (const effect of effectiveEffects) { ... applyCardEffect(...) }`), mutating `state` in place. The first dot writes the live pool at line 543: `case 'poison': state.poisonStacks += appliedValue;` (appliedValue is the base 3 + int scale, always >=3 — poison has no immunity/affinity reduction path; it is added flat). When the loop advances to effect[1], its gate evaluates `enemy_has_stack` at lines 207-210: `const stacks = readStackCount(state, cond.enemy_has_stack, 'enemy'); if (!cond.per_stack) { if (stacks <= 0) return; }`, and readStackCount line 928 returns `state.poisonStacks` — already >=3. So `stacks <= 0` is never true on a played card; the bonus 2 poison always applies. The card unconditionally applies 5 poison (3+2) even with 0 pre-existing poison, exactly as the evidence sim shows. The condition is a dead/cosmetic gate that misleads the player into thinking a precondition exists. This is NOT intended-by-design under C1-C5 (those cover power levers, not a phantom gate); the codebase already has the correct pattern for a real precondition (`enemy_stack_atleast: {stack, value}`, lines 241-244) which this card should use if a genuine threshold is wanted.
verdict.corrected_detail: Effects[0] = dot 3 poison aoe (int per3, unconditional); Effects[1] = dot 2 poison aoe (int per4) gated by enemy_has_stack:poison. CardResolver runs effects in order within a single cast and effect[0] mutates state.poisonStacks (line 543) BEFORE effect[1]'s gate reads that same field (lines 208-210 -> readStackCount line 928). Since poison application is never reduced to 0, the gate's only failure path (stacks <= 0) can never trigger on a played card, so the 'If enemy has [poison]' clause always fires. The card always applies 5 poison. Fix: fold into a flat 5 (3+2) application, or change the gate to `enemy_stack_atleast: {stack:'poison', value:N}` with N>3 so the bonus genuinely depends on PRE-EXISTING poison.

finding.title: Vein Splitter 'If enemy has [bleed]' per-hit bonus always fires (self-satisfied gate)
finding.detail: Effects: [damage 4 x3 ([dex]); dot 1 bleed per_hit ([dex]); dot 1 bleed per_hit ([dex]) condition enemy_has_stack:bleed]. The second effect applies 1 bleed per hit (3 total) unconditionally; the third effect's gate enemy_has_stack:bleed reads the live bleed pool, which is already 3 by the time it runs, so it always fires for all per_hit reps. The card therefore always applies 6 bleed — the 'if enemy has [bleed]' clause never actually gates anything on a played card.
finding.evidence: Sim, 0 pre-bleed DEX1: dmg=12, after=6 bleed (3 base + 3 'bonus'). Description: "Deal 4([dex]) three times. Each hit applies 1[bleed]([dex]). If enemy has [bleed]: each hit applies 1 more [bleed]([dex])."
finding.suggested_fix: Either make the bonus depend on PRE-EXISTING bleed (snapshot the bleed count before this card's own application, or gate on enemy_stack_atleast with a threshold above 3) so the conditional is real, or remove the clause and state a flat 2 bleed per hit.

verdict.title: Vein Splitter 'If enemy has [bleed]' per-hit bonus always fires (self-satisfied gate)
verdict.reason: CONFIRMED. Card t3-agility-attack-counter (cards.json:2630-2686) has effects[] in this order: (1) damage 4 multi_hit:2; (2) dot 1 bleed, per_hit:true; (3) dot 1 bleed, per_hit:true, condition.enemy_has_stack:bleed. In CardResolver.resolve the effects[] are iterated sequentially in array order (lines 139-147), mutating state in place. After the damage effect, lastMultiHitReps=3 (line 145), so the two per_hit dot effects each run 3 reps (line 140). Effect #2 unconditionally pushes 3 bleed onto state.bleedStacks (case 'dot' -> line 544 'state.bleedStacks += appliedValue'). Effect #3's condition is then evaluated per-rep in applyCardEffect at lines 207-210: 'const stacks = readStackCount(state, cond.enemy_has_stack, "enemy"); if (!cond.per_stack) { if (stacks <= 0) return; }'. readStackCount reads the LIVE state.bleedStacks (line 933), which effect #2 already raised to >=3. So the gate's 'stacks <= 0' is always false and the bonus bleed always applies, even with 0 pre-existing bleed. There is NO pre-application snapshot for conditions: the only snapshot (preConsume, line 132) feeds consume_stack_value exclusively, not the condition predicate. The stored description "...If enemy has [bleed]: each hit applies 1 more [bleed]([dex])." (generated via CardText.ts line 196 'If enemy has [stack]' prefix + line 382 'each hit deals/applies N more') matches the effects[] but promises a conditional that is in practice dead — on a freshly played card it always delivers 6 bleed at DEX1 (3 base + 3 "bonus"). This is a genuine dead_effect / unclear_desc, not a C1-C5 intended choice and not a resolver misread. Keeping severity med: the clause is functionally dead and the description is misleading, though the card still does deliver damage/bleed (it is not value-zero), so not high.
verdict.corrected_detail: The conditional third effect (dot 1 bleed, per_hit, condition.enemy_has_stack:bleed) is gated on a bleed pool that the card's own second effect has already populated. Because effects[] resolve sequentially in array order and the condition reads live state.bleedStacks (CardResolver lines 207-210 -> readStackCount line 933), the gate is satisfied by this card's own preceding 3-bleed application. The "If enemy has [bleed]" clause therefore never gates anything on a played card: it fires on every rep regardless of pre-existing bleed, so the card always applies 6 bleed at DEX1. Fix: either snapshot the enemy bleed count BEFORE this card's effects apply and gate on that, or gate on enemy_stack_atleast with a threshold above 3, or drop the clause and state a flat 2 bleed per hit.

finding.title: Crimson Cascade on-kill bleed-spread payoff is a no-op in single-enemy combat
finding.detail: The aura trigger on_kill_with_stack:bleed fires from CombatEngine.fireOnKillWithStack(), which runs inside checkEndConditions AFTER enemyHP<=0. Its payload is a dot apply 4 bleed to enemy_nearest. applyTriggeredPayload just adds to state.bleedStacks (the single enemy pool), but that enemy is already dead and combat ends on the same tick, so the 4 bleed never ticks. CombatEngine's own comment notes this is 'mostly a no-op until multi-enemy arrives'. The description promises a recurring kill-spread payoff that cannot trigger in the current engine.
finding.evidence: Description: "...For 15 seconds, every time you kill an enemy with [bleed], apply 4[bleed]([spi]) to the nearest enemy." CombatEngine fireOnKillWithStack fires after enemyHP=0; applyTriggeredPayload (StatusEffects, case dot) only mutates the dead enemy's stack pool.
finding.suggested_fix: Until multi-enemy combat exists, either give this card a payoff that lands during combat (e.g. an on_hit/on_stack_threshold bleed amplifier) or flag the on-kill clause as a future-content feature so players are not paying tier-3 cost/cooldown for a dead clause.

verdict.title: Crimson Cascade on-kill bleed-spread payoff is a no-op in single-enemy combat
verdict.reason: Confirmed by exact code. The aura effect (cards.json:4228-4246) has trigger "on_kill_with_stack", threshold_stack "bleed", then = dot 4 bleed to target "enemy_nearest". CombatEngine.fireOnKillWithStack() is invoked ONLY inside checkEndConditions() on the enemyHP<=0 branch (CombatEngine.ts:618-627), and the very next lines set this.state.enemyHP=0 then this.stats.result='victory' and this.isFinished=true (628-629). Every tick caller short-circuits via `if (this.checkEndConditions()) return;` (lines 97/100/103/106) and the DoT loop bails too (line 116), so once a kill registers no further tick runs. The payload itself (applyTriggeredPayload, StatusEffects.ts:389-401, dot/stack case) does only `state.bleedStacks += value` against the single shared enemy bleed pool — effect.target ("enemy_nearest") is never read anywhere in src/systems/combat (grep returns zero matches), so there is no second/live target to receive it. Result: the 4 bleed is added to a corpse's pool on the final tick and tickActiveDoTs never runs again to convert it to damage. The engine's own comment (CombatEngine.ts:622-626) states the cascade hook is "mostly a no-op until multi-enemy arrives." This is not an intended C1-C5 design choice; the stored description ("...every time you kill an enemy with [bleed], apply 4[bleed] to the nearest enemy.") promises a recurring kill-spread payoff that cannot fire in the current single-enemy engine. Severity low because the card still delivers its upfront 3[bleed]+scaling and 1 self-bleed; only the second/third clause is dead.
verdict.corrected_detail: The on_kill_with_stack aura (cards.json:4228-4246) is a dead clause in single-enemy combat. fireOnKillWithStack() runs in checkEndConditions() only after enemyHP<=0, immediately before isFinished=true (CombatEngine.ts:618-629); the tick then short-circuits everywhere. applyTriggeredPayload's dot case (StatusEffects.ts:389-401) merely does state.bleedStacks += value into the single shared enemy pool and ignores target "enemy_nearest" entirely (no references in src/systems/combat). So 4 bleed is added to the already-dead enemy on the terminal tick and never gets a chance to tick into damage. The card's upfront 3[bleed]([spi]) + 1 self-bleed still work; only the 15s kill-spread payoff is inert.

finding.target: Pyre-keyword burn detonators (t3-attack-fire-fire Cinderlance, t3-agility-fire-fire Cinder Sprint, t3-counter-fire-water Venom Detonation)
finding.title: Redundant explicit burn-consume after Pyre auto-consume; also suppresses stack_consumed event
finding.detail: These cards carry both a Pyre-keyword detonator (damage condition enemy_has_stack:burn + per_stack) AND an explicit [stack -99 burn consume_stack] effect. The CardResolver Pyre semantic sets state.burnStacks = 0 immediately after the gated damage fires (lines 307-321), so the later explicit consume_stack always operates on 0 burn — it removes nothing and emits no stack_consumed event. The explicit consume is dead (harmless), but a side effect is that these cards never emit a stack_consumed event, so they can never advance Quench Lance's (t3-fire-fire-water) 'if you consume 10+ [burn]' event_counter. Only the consume_stack_value burn cards that use a real explicit consume (Supernova, Tremor Detonate) emit stack_consumed for burn. Worth confirming this is intended.
finding.evidence: Sim: Cinderlance 10 burn -> dmg=46 (16 base + 3*10 detonate), after burn=0. The explicit [stack -99 burn consume_stack] sees burn already 0 (Pyre zeroed it). bumpEventCounters('stack_consumed') only fires when consumed>0 (CardResolver lines 619-621); convert_stack consumption (Brine Crucible) likewise never emits stack_consumed.
finding.suggested_fix: Remove the redundant explicit consume from Pyre-keyword cards (it is dead) and/or emit a stack_consumed event from the Pyre auto-consume path so Quench Lance's counter can be fed by Pyre detonations too, if that synergy is intended.

verdict.target: Pyre-keyword burn detonators (t3-attack-fire-fire Cinderlance, t3-agility-fire-fire Cinder Sprint, t3-counter-fire-water Venom Detonation) + Quench Lance synergy
verdict.title: Redundant explicit burn-consume after Pyre auto-consume is dead; Pyre detonations emit no stack_consumed event, so they cannot feed Quench Lance
verdict.reason: Verified against code/data. (1) cards.json: Cinderlance (line 5489-5505), Cinder Sprint (6411-6431), Venom Detonation (7060-7080) each have a Pyre detonator damage effect with condition {enemy_has_stack:"burn", per_stack:true} immediately followed by a separate {type:"stack", value:-99, stack:"burn", consume_stack:true}. (2) Effects run in array order (CardResolver.ts:139). (3) The Pyre auto-consume sets state.burnStacks = 0 right after the detonator damage fires (CardResolver.ts:307-321). (4) The later explicit consume then calls consumeFrom(state.burnStacks) on an already-zeroed pool (line 604): take = Math.min(0, 99) = 0, so the local `consumed` stays 0 — the consume removes nothing (dead/harmless). (5) Because consumed is 0, the stack_consumed emit is skipped (line 619 `if (consumed > 0)`), so these three cards never emit stack_consumed and thus can never advance Quench Lance's (t3-fire-fire-water, lines 2915-2935) event_counter {event:"stack_consumed", filter:{stack:"burn", min_amount:10}}. (6) Contrast confirmed: Supernova (t3-fire-fire-fire) and Tremor Detonate (t3-attack-fire-water) scale damage via consume_stack_value:"burn" (pre-consume snapshot at line 285) rather than the Pyre per_stack condition, so the auto-consume does NOT fire, burn survives to their explicit consume_stack effect, consumed>0, and stack_consumed IS emitted (line 620) — they are valid Quench Lance feeders. (7) convert_stack consumption (Brine Crucible) routes through addStack(..., -consumed) (line 753), and addStack only emits stack_applied for amount>0 (line 909), never stack_consumed — also confirmed. Severity is genuinely low: damage output is correct, the dead consume is harmless, and Quench Lance still has working feeders; the only real loss is the Pyre+Quench-Lance synergy not connecting, which reads as an oversight, not an engine misread.
verdict.corrected_detail: Confirmed as authored. For the three Pyre-keyword cards, the explicit [stack -99 burn consume_stack] is dead code: the Pyre auto-consume (CardResolver.ts:320) zeroes state.burnStacks before that effect runs, so consumeFrom sees 0, removes nothing, and—because the stack_consumed emit is gated on consumed>0 (line 619)—fires no event. Consequence: Pyre detonations cannot advance Quench Lance's stack_consumed/burn/min_amount:10 event_counter. Only consume_stack_value-style detonators (Supernova, Tremor Detonate), whose burn survives to the explicit consume, emit stack_consumed for burn and can feed Quench Lance. convert_stack consumption (Brine Crucible) likewise never emits stack_consumed (addStack emits only on positive amount, line 909). Fix options: drop the redundant explicit consume from the three Pyre cards, and/or emit a stack_consumed event from the Pyre auto-consume path (lines 307-321) if the Pyre+Quench-Lance synergy is intended.

finding.title: Vengeful Pyre's "Exhaust the next card in order" does nothing (exhaust_next never implemented)
finding.detail: Vengeful Pyre's middle clause is the devour effect {"type":"devour","value":1,"target":"self_deck","devour":{"exhaust_next":true}}. In CardResolver the devour case is the very first line `if (!rawEffect?.devour?.from_deck) break;` (CardResolver.ts:796). This effect has NO `from_deck`, so the resolver breaks out immediately and applies nothing. `exhaust_next` is never read anywhere in combat (grep shows it only in CardText.ts prose and types.ts docs, which literally say "Engine work outstanding"). So the printed clause "Exhaust the next card in order" is a no-op in real play.
finding.evidence: effects[1] = {"type":"devour","value":1,"target":"self_deck","devour":{"exhaust_next":true}}; CardResolver.ts:796 `if (!rawEffect?.devour?.from_deck) break;`; types.ts:178-181 comment "`exhaust_next` is the Vengeful Pyre variant ... Engine work outstanding."; grep finds no consumer of exhaust_next in CombatEngine/CardResolver.
finding.suggested_fix: Either implement exhaust_next (mark the next deck slot in state.devouredSlots / a skip flag so advanceDeckPointer skips it), or, if Exhaust-next is deferred, remove the devour effect and its prose clause from the card so the description doesn't promise an effect the engine can't realize.

verdict.title: Vengeful Pyre's "Exhaust the next card in order" does nothing (exhaust_next never implemented)
verdict.reason: Confirmed by exact code. Vengeful Pyre's effects[1] is {"type":"devour","value":1,"target":"self_deck","devour":{"exhaust_next":true}} (cards.json:4172-4178) with NO from_deck flag. The resolver's devour case opens with `if (!rawEffect?.devour?.from_deck) break;` (CardResolver.ts:796), so it breaks immediately and applies nothing — it neither populates state.devouredSlots nor touches the play queue. CombatEngine.advanceDeckPointer (CombatEngine.ts:537-547) only skips indices in devouredSlots and contains no reference to exhaust_next/exhaustNext. A repo-wide grep finds exhaust_next ONLY in types.ts:181 (doc comment that literally says "Engine work outstanding"), CardText.ts:855 (prose generation), and cards.json:4176 (the data itself) — there is no engine consumer. Therefore the stored description's middle clause "Exhaust the next card in order" (cards.json:4156) is a real no-op: a desc_mismatch/dead_effect. Note for calibration: the card's other two clauses still function — effects[0] grants 3 rage and effects[2] is the rest-of-combat "double rage gained" aura — so only one of three clauses is dead. The card is not fully broken, but it does print a promise the engine cannot realize, so high severity is defensible. Auditor did not misread; this is not a C1-C5 intended-design case (it's an admitted-outstanding implementation gap).
verdict.corrected_detail: effects[1] = {"type":"devour","value":1,"target":"self_deck","devour":{"exhaust_next":true}} has no from_deck, so CardResolver.ts:796 (`if (!rawEffect?.devour?.from_deck) break;`) exits the devour case before doing anything. CombatEngine has no exhaust_next consumer (advanceDeckPointer at 537-547 only skips devouredSlots). The clause "Exhaust the next card in order" is dead. The card's rage gain (effects[0]) and the "double rage gained" aura (effects[2]) DO work, so only the middle clause is dead. Fix: implement exhaust_next (e.g., flag the next play-queue/deck slot so advanceDeckPointer skips it once) or remove the devour effect + its prose clause so the description stops promising an unrealizable effect.

finding.title: "Double all [rage] gained" only doubles DIRECT rage gains, not rage from triggers/ticks/Brace/low-HP
finding.detail: Vengeful Pyre arms an aura modifier {kind:'stack_gain_mult', stack:'rage', value:1} → rendered "double all [rage] gained". The doubling lives only in CardResolver's direct `stack`/`dot` paths (which call sumStackGainMult, e.g. CardResolver.ts:627-628). Rage granted via aura `then` payloads routes through StatusEffects.applyTriggeredPayload, whose stack/dot case does `state.rageStacks += value` with NO sumStackGainMult call (confirmed: applyTriggeredPayload contains no reference to sumStackGainMult). So every rage-via-trigger source is silently NOT doubled: Wrathshell Vow's 2s tick rage, Wrath Squall's per-hit (hp_lost) rage, Stonewrath/Tombrage/Tombplate low-HP rage, Bogwrath/Bulwark Vow Brace rage. These are the rage archetype's core generators, so the central payoff of the dedicated rage-doubling card is only half-real.
finding.evidence: effects[2].modifier = {kind:'stack_gain_mult', stack:'rage', value:1}; CardResolver stack-case applies it (lines 627-628) but StatusEffects.applyTriggeredPayload stack/dot case (StatusEffects.ts:389-401) adds rage directly with no gain-mult; affected payload-rage cards: Wrathshell Vow (tick), Wrath Squall (event_counter), Stonewrath/Tombrage/Tombplate (on_hp_pct_below), Bogwrath (on_armor_break).
finding.suggested_fix: In applyTriggeredPayload's stack/dot case, apply sumStackGainMult(state.heroAuras, which) to positive gains exactly as the CardResolver stack case does, so "double all [rage] gained" covers triggered/tick/Brace rage.

verdict.title: "Double all [rage] gained" only doubles directly-played rage, not rage from triggers/ticks/Brace/low-HP payloads
verdict.reason: Confirmed at the code level by reading both resolution paths. Vengeful Pyre (cards.json:4154-4189) effects[2] arms an aura {kind:'stack_gain_mult', stack:'rage', value:1}, rendered "double all [rage] gained". The doubling is applied ONLY in CardResolver's direct play path: the `stack` case (CardResolver.ts:627-628) does `const sgm = sumStackGainMult(state.heroAuras, which); const v = resolvedValue>0 && sgm!==0 ? floor(resolvedValue*(1+sgm)) : resolvedValue` then `case 'rage': state.rageStacks += v`, and the `dot` case (CardResolver.ts:512-513) mirrors it. But rage delivered through aura `then` payloads routes through StatusEffects.applyTriggeredPayload, whose stack/dot case (StatusEffects.ts:389-401) does `case 'rage': state.rageStacks += value` with NO sumStackGainMult call. Grep confirms sumStackGainMult is referenced ONLY in CardResolver.ts (lines 512, 627) and its definition (StatusEffects.ts:188) — there is no second doubling path. Every triggered/tick/event_counter payload goes through applyTriggeredPayload (CombatEngine.ts:131,133,238,671; CardResolver.ts:525,640,621), so it is silently NOT doubled. Verified the named generator cards all deliver rage via such payloads: Wrathshell Vow (t3-counter-counter-defense) tick_ms aura then→stack rage; Wrath Squall (t3-air-counter-counter) event_counter hp_lost then→stack rage; Stonewrath (t3-counter-counter-earth) on_hp_pct_below then→stack rage; Tombrage (t3-counter-earth-earth) on_hp_pct_below then→stack rage; Tombplate (t3-counter-defense-earth) on_hp_pct_below then→stack rage; Bogwrath (t3-counter-earth-water) on_armor_break then→stack rage; Bulwark Vow (t2-defense-defense) on_armor_break then→stack rage. None of these get doubled. This is a genuine card_bug (the engine cannot realize what the text claims), not a C1-C5 intentional choice — the rage archetype is intended (Fury, flat dmg/stack), and a dedicated T3 doubler whose effect misses every archetype generator defeats its stated purpose. Kept at high because the central payoff of the card is only half-real across the archetype it exists to support. Suggested fix stands: in applyTriggeredPayload's stack/dot case, multiply positive gains by (1 + sumStackGainMult(state.heroAuras, which)) exactly as the CardResolver stack case does.
verdict.corrected_detail: The finding cites the modifier living "in CardResolver's applyTriggeredPayload" — minor location error: applyTriggeredPayload is defined in StatusEffects.ts (line 339), not CardResolver.ts (CardResolver only imports and calls it). The substance is correct: the stack/dot case is at StatusEffects.ts:389-401 and omits sumStackGainMult.

finding.title: Dust Plague's stun-at-5-slow threshold never fires from its own slow ticks
finding.detail: Dust Plague's only slow source is its tick aura ({tick_ms:2500, then: apply 1[slow]}). Tick `then` payloads are applied via StatusEffects.applyTriggeredPayload, whose dot/stack case does NOT call checkEnemyStackThreshold (the threshold check is only wired into CardResolver's dot/stack/addStack paths — CardResolver.ts:574,657,912). So when the ticking slow crosses 5, the on_enemy_stack_threshold stun aura is never evaluated. The card reads as a self-contained combo ("every 2.5s apply 1[slow]... If enemy has 5+ [slow]: apply 1[stun]"), but the stun can only proc if some OTHER slow card crosses the enemy past 5 while this aura is alive. Driven by its own ticks, the stun clause is effectively dead.
finding.evidence: effects[1] tick aura then = apply 1[slow] (routed via applyTriggeredPayload, no threshold check); effects[2] = {trigger:'on_enemy_stack_threshold', threshold_stack:'slow', threshold:5, then: apply 1[stun]}; checkEnemyStackThreshold call-sites are all in CardResolver (574/657/912) — none in applyTriggeredPayload (StatusEffects.ts:389-401).
finding.suggested_fix: Have applyTriggeredPayload (or the tick-collection loop in CombatEngine) run checkEnemyStackThreshold/checkSelfStackThreshold after applying a stack from a tick/trigger payload, mirroring the CardResolver dot/stack path; or apply the ticking slow through a code path that runs the threshold check.

verdict.title: Dust Plague's stun-at-5-slow threshold never fires from its own slow ticks
verdict.reason: Confirmed by reading the code. Dust Plague effects[1] is a tick aura {tick_ms:2500, then:{type:'dot', value:1, target:'aoe', stack:'slow'}}; effects[2] is {trigger:'on_enemy_stack_threshold', threshold_stack:'slow', threshold:5, then:{dot stun}}. In CombatEngine.ts:130-133 the per-tick payloads are applied via applyTriggeredPayload(state, e) with NO follow-up threshold check. In StatusEffects.ts:389-401 applyTriggeredPayload's dot/stack case does only `state.slowStacks += value` and the function returns at line 431 without ever calling checkEnemyStackThreshold. The ONLY call-sites of checkEnemyStackThreshold are in CardResolver.ts: line 574 (card dot/stack resolution), line 912 (addStack), and a guarded variant; line 657 is actually checkSelfStackThreshold. The tick path reaches none of these — and the dot/stack branch of applyTriggeredPayload deliberately bypasses addStack (CardResolver.ts:908 comment: "dot/stack cases do NOT call addStack"). Therefore, when Dust Plague's own ticking slow crosses the enemy from 4 to 5+, the on_enemy_stack_threshold stun aura (effects[2]) is never evaluated. The stun clause can only proc if a DIFFERENT card resolves a slow through CardResolver while effects[2]'s 12s aura is still alive. Self-driven, the advertised stun payoff is dead. This is NOT a C1-C5 intended design choice; it is a wiring gap between the tick path and the threshold dispatch. (Auditor's exact line numbers were slightly imprecise — 657 is checkSelfStackThreshold, not checkEnemyStackThreshold — but the core claim is accurate.)
verdict.corrected_detail: Dust Plague's only self-supplied slow source is its tick aura (effects[1]: tick_ms 2500, then = dot 1 slow to aoe). Tick payloads are applied by CombatEngine.ts:133 via applyTriggeredPayload, whose dot/stack case (StatusEffects.ts:389-401) just increments state.slowStacks and returns (line 431) with no threshold dispatch. checkEnemyStackThreshold is only called from CardResolver.ts (line 574 in the card dot/stack path, and line 912 inside addStack). The tick path never reaches addStack (CardResolver.ts:908 explicitly notes dot/stack cases do not call addStack), so crossing 5 slow via the card's own ticks never evaluates effects[2] (on_enemy_stack_threshold -> apply 1 stun). The stun clause only fires if another card pushes the enemy past 5 slow through CardResolver while the 12s threshold aura is alive; driven by its own ticks it is effectively dead. Fix: have the CombatEngine tick loop (or applyTriggeredPayload) run checkEnemyStackThreshold/checkSelfStackThreshold after applying a stack from a tick/trigger payload, mirroring the CardResolver dot/stack path.

finding.title: "For 15 seconds, if you have less than X%[HP]: ..." reads as repeatable but fires once
finding.detail: These on_hp_pct_below auras fire exactly ONCE and self-remove (StatusEffects.fireHpThresholdTriggers splices the aura after firing, lines 142-147). The prose "For 15 seconds, if you have less than 40%[HP], gain 8[rage]" reads like a sustained condition that pays out whenever it's true during the window, but the payoff lands only on the first HP-loss event that leaves the hero below the threshold; the aura is then gone. A player cannot tell from the text that it is a one-shot armed trigger rather than a recurring/while-true effect.
finding.evidence: e.g. Tombrage effects[1] = {trigger:'on_hp_pct_below', threshold:40, ttl_ms:15000, then: gain 8[rage]}; StatusEffects.ts:142-147 fireHpThresholdTriggers does `auras.splice(i,1)` after firing (fires once).
finding.suggested_fix: Reword to convey single-shot arming, e.g. "For 15 seconds, the first time you drop below 40%[HP], gain 8[rage]"; or make these triggers repeatable (don't splice) if the intent is a recurring payoff.

verdict.title: "For 15 seconds, if you have less than X%[HP]: ..." reads as a sustained while-true condition but the aura arms once and self-removes on the first trigger
verdict.reason: Confirmed real after re-reading code and data. PROSE: CardText.ts formatTriggerAura (lines 782-792) emits `${dur}, ${trigPhrase}, ${body}` for on_hp_pct_below auras, and auraTriggerPhrase (lines 713-715) returns `if you have less than ${th}%[HP]`. This reproduces the stored descriptions verbatim (e.g. Tombrage: "For 15 seconds, if you have less than 40%[HP], gain 8[rage]"; Stonewrath: 50%/6 rage; Tombplate: 40%/10 armor+4 rage; Quickstone: 60%/haste 30% 6s). No wording signals a one-shot arm. MECHANIC: StatusEffects.ts fireHpThresholdTriggers (lines 142-147) pushes the then-payload(s) and then unconditionally `auras.splice(i,1)` — fires exactly once; the doc comment (line 137) confirms "Triggered auras self-remove." It is driven each frame from CardResolver (fireHpThresholdTriggers at CardResolver call site / tmp_resolver.mjs:1575) and EnemyAI.ts:342, so once spliced the aura is gone for the rest of the 15s window. The repo's own test tier2-primitives.test.ts:160-166 proves this: after firing at 30% HP, a second check at 25% returns 0 payloads. So the prose ("For 15 seconds, if you have less than X%") implies a recurring/while-true payout during the window, but the engine pays out only on the first HP-loss event crossing the threshold, then the aura is consumed — a player cannot infer this from the text. Not a C1-C5 intentional design point (those cover resource/STR/DoT/control tuning, not trigger wording), and the resolver was read correctly, not misread. Confirming at LOW: the effect does fire as a number-correct payload (8 rage etc.) — the defect is clarity/expectation about WHEN/HOW OFTEN it fires, not a dead or numerically-wrong effect.
verdict.corrected_detail: All four cards arm an on_hp_pct_below aura (ttl_ms 15000) that fires its `then` payload exactly once and self-removes (StatusEffects.ts:142-147 splices the aura after pushing the payload). The generated prose "For 15 seconds, if you have less than X%[HP], <effect>" reads as a sustained condition that should pay out whenever HP is below the threshold during the window, but in the engine it lands only on the FIRST frame the hero is detected below threshold, after which the aura is gone for the remaining window. Thresholds/payloads as stored: Stonewrath 50% -> 6 rage; Tombrage 40% -> 8 rage; Tombplate 40% -> 10 armor + 4 rage (two separate one-shot auras); Quickstone 60% -> spawns a 6s cd_reduction 0.3 ("haste 30%") aura. Suggested reword to convey single-shot arming, e.g. "For 15 seconds, the first time you drop below 40%[HP], gain 8[rage]"; or make the trigger repeatable (do not splice in fireHpThresholdTriggers) if a recurring payout is intended. Note: the same pattern exists on t3-fire-water-water (Phoenix Aura, 50% -> 18 armor) and other T3 "relief" auras, so a global fix to the prose template in formatTriggerAura would cover all of them.

finding.title: Stun applied via trigger payloads bypasses the stun diminishing-returns (immunity) window
finding.detail: CardResolver gates stun application on the DR window (`if (state.combatElapsedMs >= state.stunImmuneUntilMs) state.stunStacks += ...` at CardResolver.ts:548 and 634). But stun applied through StatusEffects.applyTriggeredPayload (Stoneward Reprisal's Brace stun, Dust Plague's threshold stun) does `state.stunStacks += value` directly with no stunImmuneUntilMs check (applyTriggeredPayload has no reference to stunImmune). So Brace/threshold stun can re-apply during the resist window the engine otherwise enforces to prevent perma-stun. This is an internal inconsistency (it helps the player rather than misrepresenting the card), but it means the anti-perma-chain rule (D-9) is not uniformly applied.
finding.evidence: CardResolver.ts:548 `case 'stun': if (state.combatElapsedMs >= (state.stunImmuneUntilMs ?? 0)) state.stunStacks += appliedValue;` vs StatusEffects.ts:396 `case 'stun': state.stunStacks += value;` (no window check); Stoneward Reprisal Brace then=apply 2[stun]; Dust Plague threshold then=apply 1[stun].
finding.suggested_fix: Apply the same stunImmuneUntilMs gate in applyTriggeredPayload's stun branch so trigger/Brace/threshold stun respects the DR window like the main resolver path.

verdict.title: Stun applied via trigger payloads bypasses the stun diminishing-returns (immunity) window
verdict.reason: Confirmed against the actual code. The DR mechanism exists: CombatEngine.ts:498-502 sets state.stunImmuneUntilMs = combatElapsedMs + STUN_IMMUNE_MS when a freeze fully ends, and the comment (lines 495-497) states this is to prevent perma-chaining (D-9). The MAIN resolver path honors it at CardResolver.ts:548 (`case 'stun': if (state.combatElapsedMs >= (state.stunImmuneUntilMs ?? 0)) state.stunStacks += appliedValue;`) and again at :634 for the bare `stack` effect. But the TRIGGER-PAYLOAD path does NOT: applyTriggeredPayload (StatusEffects.ts:339) handles dot/stack at lines 389-401 with `case 'stun': state.stunStacks += value;` (line 396) and contains no reference to stunImmuneUntilMs anywhere. Both cited cards route stun through this unguarded path: Stoneward Reprisal (t3-defense-defense-earth) arms an on_armor_break aura with then={type:dot, stack:stun, value:2} (cards.json:3962-3974), which fires via EnemyAI.ts:299-301 fireTrigger->applyTriggeredPayload; Dust Plague (t3-air-air-earth) arms an on_enemy_stack_threshold aura with then={type:dot, stack:stun, value:1, scale int} (cards.json:3309-3328), which fires via checkEnemyStackThreshold (CardResolver.ts:864-878) ending in applyTriggeredPayload(state,e) at line 878. So Brace/threshold stun can land during the resist window the engine otherwise enforces. This is a genuine internal inconsistency with the D-9 anti-perma-chain rule (not an intended C1-C5 design choice — slow/stun being control-only is unrelated to the DR gate). It is not a desc_mismatch (the card text never mentions the DR window), and it helps the player rather than misrepresenting the card, so low severity is appropriate. Suggested fix is sound: gate the stun branch in applyTriggeredPayload on state.combatElapsedMs >= (state.stunImmuneUntilMs ?? 0) to match the resolver.
verdict.corrected_detail: data_inconsistency (low): applyTriggeredPayload (StatusEffects.ts:396) applies `case 'stun': state.stunStacks += value;` with no stunImmuneUntilMs gate, whereas the main resolver enforces the DR window at CardResolver.ts:548 and :634. Stoneward Reprisal's on_armor_break stun (fired via EnemyAI.ts:301) and Dust Plague's on_enemy_stack_threshold stun (fired via checkEnemyStackThreshold->applyTriggeredPayload, CardResolver.ts:878) therefore re-apply stun during the post-freeze resist window, partially defeating the D-9 anti-perma-stun guard. Fix: add the same `if (state.combatElapsedMs >= (state.stunImmuneUntilMs ?? 0))` gate to the stun branch in applyTriggeredPayload.

finding.title: Galekick over-efficient versus its sibling Tempest Pike (same slow payload, more damage, shorter cooldown)
finding.detail: Galekick (air-attack-attack, cd 1.6): Deal 7([str]) twice (14 base) + apply 2[slow]. Tempest Pike (air-air-attack, cd 2.2): Deal 6([str]) twice (12 base) + apply 2[slow] — same slow, less damage, longer cooldown. Galekick delivers more burst AND more slow-per-second on a noticeably shorter cooldown for the same archetype role, making Tempest Pike strictly worse as a slow-applier. Noting as a light balance outlier (sim-based tuning handled elsewhere).
finding.evidence: Galekick: [{damage 7 x2 (str/2)},{dot slow 2}] cd 1.6. Tempest Pike: [{damage 6 x2 (str/2)},{dot slow 2}] cd 2.2.
finding.suggested_fix: Nudge Galekick's cooldown up or its damage down (or differentiate the two cards' slow/damage split) so the two air slow-attackers occupy distinct power points.

verdict.title: Galekick over-efficient versus its sibling Tempest Pike (same slow payload, more damage, shorter cooldown)
verdict.reason: Data confirmed by direct read of cards.json. Galekick (t3-air-attack-attack, cards.json:3718): description "Deal 7([str]) twice. Apply 2[slow].", effects [{damage 7, multi_hit 1, scale str per2 val1},{dot slow 2}], cost {stamina 2}, cooldown 1.6, targeting aoe. Tempest Pike (t3-air-air-attack, cards.json:5559): description "Deal 6([str]) twice. Apply 2[slow].", effects [{damage 6, multi_hit 1, scale str per2 val1},{dot slow 2}], cost {stamina 2}, cooldown 2.2, targeting aoe. CardResolver.ts:445 confirms multi_hit:1 => 1+1=2 hits, so Galekick = 14 base dmg, Tempest Pike = 12 base dmg. Same 2[slow] payload, same 2 stamina cost, same scale. Galekick delivers more burst (14 vs 12) AND more slow-per-second on a SHORTER cooldown (1.6 vs 2.2), so as a slow-applier Tempest Pike is strictly dominated by Galekick. This is a real numeric/cooldown dominance, not a misread. It is genuinely a relative-balance outlier rather than a per-card bug or desc-mismatch, so it stays low severity per the rubric (sim-based balance handled elsewhere). One caveat: Galekick is more attack-weighted in elements (air-attack-attack vs air-air-attack), so higher damage is plausibly intended, but the higher-damage card also having the shorter cooldown is the part that produces strict dominance and is worth flagging lightly.
verdict.corrected_detail: Galekick (air-attack-attack, cd 1.6, aoe, 2 stamina): "Deal 7([str]) twice" = 14 base + apply 2[slow]. Tempest Pike (air-air-attack, cd 2.2, aoe, 2 stamina): "Deal 6([str]) twice" = 12 base + apply 2[slow]. Identical slow payload, cost, and str scaling, but Galekick has both more burst (14 vs 12) and a shorter cooldown (1.6 vs 2.2), giving it more slow-per-second too. As a slow-applier, Tempest Pike is strictly worse. Note Galekick's elements lean attack-heavy (extra raw damage is plausibly intended), so the cleanest differentiation is to lengthen Galekick's cooldown (or shorten/buff Tempest Pike's) so the higher-damage card is not also the more frequent one.

finding.title: Aura/triggered heals skip the universal +15%/pt SPI heal multiplier that every direct heal gets
finding.detail: The glossary states 'every heal is increased by 15% per point of SPI' and CardResolver.applyEffect 'heal' case applies that universal multiplier (spiBonus = floor(resolvedValue * spi*0.15)). But heals that fire from auras/triggers/ticks route through StatusEffects.applyTriggeredPayload, whose 'heal' case is just `state.heroHP = min(max, heroHP + value)` — it applies the explicit scale clause (lines 361-373) but NOT the universal +15%/pt bonus. So Crimson Regen Mantle's regen tick ('heal 3([spi])') and Standing Stone's Brace 'Heal 8' heal strictly less for a high-SPI hero than the identically-worded direct heals on Tidesong/Brineward/etc. A SPI-stacking sustain build silently loses the headline SPI scaling on exactly the over-time heals it builds around. The code comment at applyTriggeredPayload line 359 even says SPI 'is applied at the caller (or here for heal type)', but it is not applied in either place for this path.
finding.evidence: StatusEffects.ts applyTriggeredPayload heal case: `case 'heal': { state.heroHP = Math.min(state.heroMaxHP, state.heroHP + value); break; }` (no spiBonus). Compare CardResolver.ts heal case: `const spiBonus = Math.floor(resolvedValue * (state.heroSpirit * 0.15)); const totalHeal = resolvedValue + spiBonus;`. KeywordDefinitions.ts SPI: 'every heal is increased by 15% per point of SPI.' CombatEngine.ts line 131 routes collectAuraTicks payloads through applyTriggeredPayload.
finding.suggested_fix: In applyTriggeredPayload's 'heal' case, apply the same SPI multiplier as CardResolver (heal = value + floor(value * heroSpirit * 0.15)), so direct and over-time heals scale identically with the disclosed SPI rule.

verdict.title: Aura/triggered heals skip the universal +15%/pt SPI heal multiplier that every direct heal gets
verdict.reason: Confirmed by direct code read. CardResolver.ts heal case (lines 451-456) applies the universal SPI multiplier to every direct heal: `const spiBonus = Math.floor(resolvedValue * (state.heroSpirit * 0.15)); const totalHeal = resolvedValue + spiBonus;`. But StatusEffects.ts applyTriggeredPayload heal case (lines 406-409) does NOT: `case 'heal': { state.heroHP = Math.min(state.heroMaxHP, state.heroHP + value); break; }` — `value` only includes the explicit scale clause (lines 361-373), never the +15%/pt SPI bonus. CombatEngine.ts routes ALL aura/trigger heals through applyTriggeredPayload with no SPI pre-multiplication (line 131 aura ticks, line 238 event-counter payloads, line 671 on-kill triggers), so neither the caller nor the callee applies SPI. Card data confirms both targets hit this path: t3-counter-defense-water has an aura {tick_ms:1000, then:{type:'heal', value:3, scale:{stat:'spi',per:3,value:1}}} — its per-second regen tick; t3-air-earth-earth has an aura {trigger:'on_armor_break', then:{type:'heal', value:8}} — its Brace heal. The code comment at StatusEffects.ts line 359-360 itself claims SPI 'is applied at the caller (or here for heal type)' but it is applied in NEITHER place. This is not a C1-C5 intended choice — the design notes never exempt over-time heals from the disclosed SPI rule, and the glossary states 'every heal is increased by 15% per point of SPI.' A SPI-stacking sustain build silently loses the headline SPI scaling on exactly the over-time/triggered heals these two cards build around. Med severity: affects sustain-build power but only on a narrow set of heal paths.
verdict.corrected_detail: The auditor's mechanism and line citations are accurate. One refinement: applyTriggeredPayload's `value` DOES honor the effect's explicit `scale` clause (StatusEffects.ts lines 361-373), so Crimson Regen Mantle's tick still gets its `scale:{stat:'spi',per:3,value:1}` (+1 per 3 SPI). What it loses is the SEPARATE universal +15%/pt SPI multiplier that CardResolver layers on top of resolvedValue. Standing Stone's Heal 8 has no scale clause, so it loses SPI scaling entirely. Net: both heal strictly less than identically-worded direct heals at any SPI > 0.

finding.title: 'For 12 seconds, if you have less than 50% HP, gain 18 armor' is an edge-trigger on an HP-losing hit, not a state check
finding.detail: The text reads as a standing condition: while the buff is up, being under 50% HP grants 18 armor. The engine implements on_hp_pct_below via fireHpThresholdTriggers, which is ONLY called inside EnemyAI.applyHeroDamage and ONLY when `remaining > 0` (an enemy hit that actually removes HP). Consequences: (a) casting Phoenix Aura while already below 50% grants nothing until an enemy lands an HP-removing hit; (b) if your armor absorbs the next hit (remaining===0) it does not fire; (c) if no enemy attack lands in the 12s window it never fires. It fires once and the aura self-removes. A player cannot tell from the text that the armor only appears on the specific hit that drops them below 50% and is gated on HP loss.
finding.evidence: Card JSON: {aura ttl_ms:12000, trigger:on_hp_pct_below, threshold:50, then:{armor 18}}. StatusEffects.fireHpThresholdTriggers self-removes fired auras (auras.splice). EnemyAI.ts: fireHpThresholdTriggers called only inside `if (remaining > 0)` block (line ~340). No per-tick HP check exists for this trigger (contrast Crimson Regen Mantle which uses a per-tick condition hero_hp_pct_below:50 that IS re-checked every tick).
finding.suggested_fix: Reword to reflect the edge trigger ('the next time an enemy hit drops you below 50% HP, gain 18[armor]') OR implement on_hp_pct_below as a recurring per-tick state check during the aura's life so the text matches.

verdict.title: 'For 12 seconds, if you have less than 50% HP, gain 18 armor' is a one-shot edge trigger gated on an HP-removing hit, not the standing state check the text implies
verdict.reason: CONFIRMED. Phoenix Aura's third effect is {type:aura, ttl_ms:12000, trigger:"on_hp_pct_below", threshold:50, then:{armor 18}} (cards.json:3056-3068). The only firing path for trigger "on_hp_pct_below" is fireHpThresholdTriggers (StatusEffects.ts:139-150), which (a) fires only when heroHpPct < threshold AND (b) self-removes the aura via auras.splice(i,1) on the first fire. That function is called in exactly ONE place: EnemyAI.ts:342, which sits inside the `if (remaining > 0)` block opened at EnemyAI.ts:336 — so it fires ONLY on a hit that actually removes HP. CombatEngine only calls tickAuras (pure decay, CombatEngine.ts:124-125); there is NO per-tick HP-state re-check for on_hp_pct_below anywhere. Consequences all hold: casting while already <50% grants nothing until an HP-removing hit lands; a fully armor-absorbed hit (remaining===0) does not fire it; if nothing removes HP in 12s it never fires; it fires once then self-removes. The unclear_desc verdict is the crux: the generator for the aura trigger (CardText.ts:713-715 -> "if you have less than 50%[HP]") produces prose nearly identical to the generator for the genuine per-application state-check condition hero_hp_pct_below (CardText.ts:150-156 -> "If you have less than 50%[HP]"). The latter (e.g. Crimson Regen Mantle, cards.json:2604/2618) is re-evaluated on every application via StatusEffects.ts:345-347 / CardResolver.ts:225-227, so the SAME wording means a standing condition on those cards but a one-shot HP-loss-gated edge trigger here. A player cannot distinguish the two from the text. Not invalid under C1-C5: this isn't an intentional armor/heal balance choice, it's a text-vs-mechanic mismatch about WHEN/HOW the 18 armor is granted. Severity med is right — the effect does realize under typical combat (enemies land hits), so it's not a dead/hard-bug, but the "For 12 seconds, while under 50% HP" framing materially misstates timing and silently no-ops in the cast-while-low and armor-absorbed cases. Minor evidence nit: the gating block is at EnemyAI.ts:336 (call at 342), not exactly "~340"; and self-DoT/card self-damage also route through applyHeroDamage so the trigger isn't strictly enemy-only — but it still only fires on real HP loss, so the substance is unchanged.
verdict.corrected_detail: Phoenix Aura's "For 12 seconds, if you have less than 50%[HP], gain 18[armor]" is implemented as a one-shot edge trigger (aura trigger on_hp_pct_below) that fires only on the specific hit that actually removes HP (EnemyAI.ts:342 is inside `if (remaining > 0)` at line 336) and self-removes after firing once (StatusEffects.ts:146). There is no per-tick or per-cast HP-state re-check (CombatEngine ticks auras for decay only, CombatEngine.ts:124-125). So: (a) casting while already <50% HP grants nothing until an HP-removing hit lands; (b) a hit fully absorbed by armor (remaining===0) does not fire it; (c) if nothing removes hero HP during the 12s it never fires. The wording is misleading because the identical phrase "if you have less than 50%[HP]" is also emitted for the genuine per-application condition hero_hp_pct_below (CardText.ts:150 vs CardText.ts:713), which on cards like Crimson Regen Mantle (cards.json:2604/2618) IS re-evaluated every application (StatusEffects.ts:345-347, CardResolver.ts:225-227). Players cannot tell Phoenix Aura's clause apart from a standing state check. Fix: reword to convey the edge trigger (e.g. "the next time a hit drops you below 50% HP, gain 18[armor]") or implement on_hp_pct_below as a recurring per-tick state check for the aura's lifetime so the text matches.

finding.title: Defense card converts ALL held armor to retained, repeatable Pierce (per:1) while also gaining armor — broadest armor->damage in the set
finding.detail: Design intent: armor is 'intentionally race-LOSING (no broad armor->damage)' and the only sanctioned armor->damage finisher is Citadel Inferno, which CONSUMES all armor. Body Slam Vow (defense category, cost 1 stamina, cd 2.4s) deals '2 Pierce, +1 damage per [armor] you have' (scale.source:armor, per:1, value:1) and then 'Gain 6[armor]'. It does NOT spend the armor, so the +1/armor bonus persists and compounds every cast as a VIT/armor build stacks defense. This is a self-fueling armor->damage engine — the 'broad armor->damage' the design forbids. Aegis of Returning Wrath also converts armor->damage without consuming, but at per:4 and the sim notes (SIM-PROPOSAL-QUESTIONED.md) treat Aegis's unconditional 'Deal 6 Pierce +1/4 armor' as an intentional rewrite; Body Slam Vow's per:1 rate with self-armor-gain is far more aggressive and not flagged as intended.
finding.evidence: Card JSON: [{type:damage,value:2,pierce_armor:true,scale:{stat:vit,per:1,value:1,source:armor}},{type:armor,value:6,scale:{stat:vit,per:2,value:1}}]. CardResolver applyEffect: source:armor reads state.heroDefense; spend_armor is undefined so heroDefense is never drained. Contrast Citadel Inferno: spend_armor:'all'.
finding.suggested_fix: Either reduce the conversion rate (per:>=4) to match Aegis, make Body Slam Vow consume the armor it monetizes (like Citadel Inferno), or remove the self-armor-gain so the converter isn't self-fueling. Confirm Body Slam Vow's armor->damage is intended (Aegis is documented as intentional; Body Slam Vow is not).

verdict.title: Defense card converts ALL held armor to retained, repeatable Pierce (per:1) while also gaining armor — broadest armor->damage in the set
verdict.reason: Every technical claim is verifiable in the data/resolver. cards.json line 2115-2137: damage effect value:2, pierce_armor:true, scale{source:"armor", per:1, value:1}, then a separate armor effect value:6 — no spend_armor field on the card, no exhaust, cooldown 2.4s.
