CardResolver.ts line 370-373 reads `scale.source==="armor" ? state.heroDefense` and computes resolvedValue = value + floor(heroDefense/per)*value, i.e. +1 Pierce per point of held armor. The drain block at line 179-182 only fires on card.spend_armor, which is undefined here, so heroDefense is never reduced — the +1/armor bonus persists and compounds as the card itself adds +6 armor each cast. This is mechanically the most aggressive armor->damage converter in the set: per:1 (1:1) vs Aegis of Returning Wrath's per:4 (line 1894-1898) and vs Citadel Inferno which is per:1 BUT spend_armor:"all" + exhaust:true (line 5942-5961). It genuinely conflicts with the stated design intent ("armor ... intentionally race-LOSING, no broad armor->damage"). Aegis is explicitly sanctioned in SIM-PROPOSAL-QUESTIONED.md (line 280-281: "unconditional 'Deal 6 Pierce, +1 per 4[armor]'"); Body Slam Vow appears NOWHERE in that proposal doc, so it is an undocumented exception. Confirmed as a real balance/design-tension observation. NOT raised to med: per the audit rules balance_smells must be kept light and sim-based balance is handled elsewhere; the card's stored description honestly matches its effects (no desc_mismatch) and it works exactly as authored in the engine (no card_bug or dead_effect). Severity adjusted med->low accordingly.
verdict.corrected_detail: Body Slam Vow (defense, cost 1 stamina, cd 2.4s, no exhaust) deals "2 Pierce, +1 damage per [armor] you have" via scale{source:"armor", per:1, value:1}, then "Gain 6[armor]". The resolver (CardResolver.ts:370-373) reads state.heroDefense for the bonus but never drains it — spend_armor is undefined, so the line 179-182 drain block is skipped. Because the same card also grants +6 armor each cast, the converter is self-fueling and repeatable, making it the broadest armor->damage engine in the set. This is 4x the conversion rate of the sanctioned Aegis of Returning Wrath (per:4, documented intentional in SIM-PROPOSAL-QUESTIONED.md:280-281) and lacks the consume+exhaust guardrails that constrain Citadel Inferno (per:1 but spend_armor:"all" + exhaust). It contradicts the C-series intent that armor not broadly convert to damage, and unlike Aegis it is undocumented as an intended exception. Suggested fix: align the rate to per:>=4, add spend_armor so the converter consumes what it monetizes, or drop the self-armor-gain so it is not self-fueling. Low severity — this is a design-tension/numeric note for the balance pass, not an engine fault: the description matches the effects and the card behaves exactly as authored.

finding.title: Armor-source damage effect carries a no-op scale.stat:'vit' that the resolver ignores
finding.detail: The damage effect uses scale:{stat:'vit', per:1, value:1, source:'armor'}. The resolver's source==='armor' branch reads state.heroDefense and never consults scale.stat, so the 'vit' field does nothing here. CardText also drops the ([vit]) suffix for armor-source scales, so the description is correct ('+1 damage per [armor] you have'), but the JSON's stat:'vit' is dead metadata that can mislead future editors into thinking the per-armor rate scales with VIT.
finding.evidence: CardResolver applyEffect: `const statValue = scale.source === 'armor' ? state.heroDefense : readStat(state, scale.stat);` — stat is only read when source !== 'armor'. CardText scalerSuffix returns '' when src==='armor'.
finding.suggested_fix: Drop the stat/per/value scale fields and keep only {source:'armor', per:1, value:1} (matching Aegis's clean {source:'armor',per:4,value:1}), so the data reflects what the engine does.

verdict.title: Armor-source damage effect carries a no-op scale.stat:'vit' that the resolver ignores
verdict.reason: Confirmed on every factual claim. cards.json (lines 2117-2127) gives the damage effect scale {stat:'vit', per:1, value:1, source:'armor'}. CardResolver.ts applyEffect (lines 370-373) computes `const statValue = scale.source === 'armor' ? state.heroDefense : readStat(state, scale.stat);` — when source==='armor' it reads state.heroDefense and NEVER consults scale.stat, so 'vit' contributes nothing to the resolved value. CardText.ts scalerSuffix (lines 92-100) returns '' for src==='armor', and damageBody's armor branch (lines 348-356) hard-sets statS=''. Net: the stored description "Deal 2 Pierce, +1 damage per [armor] you have." is correct and the 'vit' field is dead metadata in both engine math and text. Not a bug or desc_mismatch; purely a data-hygiene inconsistency that could mislead a future editor into thinking the per-armor rate scales with VIT. Low severity is right.
verdict.corrected_detail: The finding is accurate but its framing of the card as an oddity vs. Aegis is misleading. A catalog scan of all six "source":"armor" scales shows five of them carry a redundant stat field (vit at the target card and at lines 955-959; str at 1124, 2020, 5943), while only one (Aegis at line 1895) uses the clean {source:'armor', per, value} form with no stat. So the target card follows the DOMINANT convention, not an anomaly. The dead-metadata observation is real and correct, but the suggested fix should be applied catalog-wide (all five armor-source scales that carry a stat), not just to this one card, if the goal is data hygiene. finding.target: Brace cards (e.g. t3-defense-defense-earth Stoneward Reprisal, t3-agility-counter-defense Bramble Step, t3-agility-defense-fire Ember Vault, t3-air-earth-earth Standing Stone, t3-agility-defense-defense Phalanx Drift)
finding.title: Brace payoff silently expires after a hidden 12s window if armor never breaks
finding.detail: Every Brace (on_armor_break) aura is created with ttl_ms:12000 (Bedrock Bulwark 15000) and is pruned by tickAuras when its TTL elapses. The card text ('Brace: Apply 2[stun]', 'Brace: Deal 10', etc.) and the Brace glossary entry ('Triggers when your armor breaks (drops to 0)') give no hint of a time limit. If the gained armor (8-22) does not deplete to exactly 0 within ~12s — common against low-damage enemies or when re-armoring — the Brace payoff is lost without feedback. The window is invisible to the player.
finding.evidence: Card JSON for all on_armor_break auras carry ttl_ms:12000 (15000 for Bedrock Bulwark). StatusEffects.tickAuras prunes when remainingMs<=0. KeywordDefinitions Brace text omits any duration.
finding.suggested_fix: Either make Brace auras combat-long (ttl_ms:null) so the keyword matches its definition, or surface the window in the card text / glossary ('for the next 12 seconds').

verdict.target: Brace (on_armor_break) cards: t2-defense-defense Bulwark Vow, t2-agility-defense (+stamina) Phalanx Drift, Stoneward Reprisal, Bramble Step, Ember Vault, Standing Stone, Bedrock Bulwark
verdict.title: Brace payoff silently expires after a hidden 12s (ttl_ms) window if armor never breaks to 0
verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED as unclear_desc (low). Code/data verified end-to-end:

1) cards.json: every on_armor_break aura is authored with a FINITE ttl_ms:12000 (e.g. Bulwark Vow lines 287-298; Phalanx Drift 699-709; Stoneward Reprisal 1338-1348; Ember Vault 2178-2189; Bramble Step 2710-2721; Standing Stone 3439-3449).

2) StatusEffects.createAura (line 60-62): `const ttl = effect.ttl_ms ?? 5000; remainingMs: ttl === null ? Infinity : ttl` — 12000 produces a finite, decaying lifetime (only ttl_ms:null would be combat-long).

3) StatusEffects.tickAuras (line 154-168): `if (Number.isFinite(a.remainingMs)) a.remainingMs -= deltaMs; ... if (Number.isFinite(a.remainingMs) && a.remainingMs <= 0) auras.splice(i,1)` — the aura is pruned ~12s after the card is played.

4) EnemyAI.ts line 294-299: the payload only fires on the exact transition `armorBefore > 0 && state.heroDefense === 0`, via fireTrigger over heroAuras. fireTrigger (StatusEffects.ts 282-289) only iterates auras still IN the list, so once tickAuras prunes the aura the Brace payload can never fire.

5) Armor does not decay over time (no armor-decay logic in CombatEngine.ts; armor only drops via enemy hits). So if incoming damage in the 12s window is less than the gained armor (8-22) — common vs low-damage enemies or when the player re-armors — armor never reaches 0 and the Brace bonus is lost with no feedback.

6) Player-facing text gives no hint of the window: KeywordDefinitions.ts line 34 'Triggers its bonus when your [armor] breaks (drops to 0)' and the Armor entry line 106 both omit any duration. Card descriptions read 'Brace: Apply 3[bleed]' / 'Brace: Heal 8' with no timer.

This is genuinely unclear, NOT intended-by-design: corroborating evidence is that the SAME cards explicitly state OTHER timed windows in prose — Standing Stone's description is 'Gain 16[armor]([vit]). Brace: Heal 8. Haste 10% for 8 seconds.' — so the engine/CardText already surfaces 'for N seconds' for the Haste aura but silently hides the 12s window on the Brace aura on the same card. A player cannot tell the Brace bonus is time-limited.

Two corrections to the prior auditor's evidence (do not affect the verdict): (a) Bedrock Bulwark's Brace aura is also ttl_ms:12000, not 15000 — its 8000 belongs to a separate cd_reduction aura; (b) the exact card set is slightly different from the names listed (verified ids above), but every on_armor_break aura in the file exhibits the issue. Severity low is right: it's a clarity/feedback gap, not a broken effect.
verdict.corrected_detail: Every Brace (on_armor_break) aura is authored with a finite ttl_ms (12000ms for all of them, including Bedrock Bulwark — the prior report's '15000' is wrong; its 8000 is a separate cd_reduction aura). createAura turns a finite ttl_ms into a decaying remainingMs, and tickAuras prunes the aura when remainingMs<=0, so the armed Brace payload disappears ~12s after the card is played. fireTrigger only fires auras still in heroAuras, and the trigger only fires on the exact armor>0 -> armor==0 transition (EnemyAI.ts 294-299). Since armor does not decay on its own, if the gained armor (8-22) is not whittled to exactly 0 by enemy hits within ~12s, the Brace bonus is silently lost. No card text or glossary entry (Brace: 'Triggers its bonus when your [armor] breaks') mentions any time limit — yet the same cards DO spell out other windows (Standing Stone: 'Haste 10% for 8 seconds'), so the omission is an inconsistency the player cannot detect.

finding.title: "If enemy has [bleed]" bonus is always true on the card's own cast (self-enabling gate)
finding.detail: Description: "Deal 4([dex]) three times. Each hit applies 1[bleed]([dex]). If enemy has [bleed]: each hit applies 1 more [bleed]([dex])." The effects[] order is: multi_hit:2 damage, then per_hit dot bleed +1 (unconditional, 3 applications), then per_hit dot bleed +1 gated on enemy_has_stack:'bleed'. The unconditional per_hit bleed block runs FIRST and sets the enemy's bleed to 3, so by the time the gated block evaluates its condition (CardResolver applyCardEffect reads readStackCount live, line 207-214), the enemy always has bleed. The gate therefore never fails. Sim on a FRESH enemy (0 bleed) yields 6 bleed, identical to casting on a pre-bleeding enemy's increment. The 'If enemy has [bleed]' clause reads as situational/conditional to a player but is unconditional in practice.
finding.evidence: Resolver applies effects in array order (CardResolver.ts:139-147); condition enemy_has_stack:'bleed' is checked per-rep against live state (lines 207-214). Sim: fresh enemy -> bleed=6; enemy with 5 pre-bleed -> bleed=11 (5 + 3 + 3). The unconditional dot (idx 1) precedes the gated dot (idx 2), guaranteeing the gate passes.
finding.suggested_fix: Either reword to reflect reality (e.g. drop the gate and say 'Each hit applies 2[bleed]([dex])', matching the always-6 outcome), or snapshot the pre-cast bleed count and gate on that (use a pre_consume/snapshot read) so the bonus genuinely requires the enemy to already be bleeding.

verdict.title: "If enemy has [bleed]" bonus is always true on the card's own cast (self-enabling gate)
verdict.reason: Confirmed by code. In cards.json (lines 2640-2678) the effects[] are ordered: (idx 0) damage value 4 with multi_hit:2 -> 3 hits; (idx 1) dot bleed +1 per_hit, UNCONDITIONAL; (idx 2) dot bleed +1 per_hit, gated on condition.enemy_has_stack:'bleed'. CardResolver.ts applies effects in strict array order (loop lines 139-147). The dot resolver mutates enemy bleed synchronously and immediately: line 544 `case 'bleed': state.bleedStacks += appliedValue;`. The gated effect's condition is evaluated against LIVE state via `readStackCount(state, cond.enemy_has_stack, 'enemy')` at line 208, with the early-return at line 210 (`if (stacks <= 0) return`). Crucially, the pre-consume snapshot (preConsume, line 132) is ONLY consumed for `consume_stack_value` detonators (lines 265-271); enemy_has_stack conditions are NOT snapshotted — they read post-mutation state. Therefore idx 1 raises bleed from 0 to >0 before idx 2 evaluates its gate, so on a fresh enemy the gate passes on all reps. Per-rep ordering confirms it: both per_hit blocks inherit lastMultiHitReps=3 (set at line 145 from the multi_hit:2 damage), and idx 1's 3 reps all run before idx 2 begins, so even idx 2's first rep sees bleed>0. The "If enemy has [bleed]" clause is therefore unconditional in practice — it reads as a situational bonus to the player but always fires. This is an unclear_desc / self-enabling-gate issue, not intended-by-design under C1-C5 (no design directive calls for a self-satisfying conditional). Severity med is appropriate: it is misleading text, not a crash, and the numeric outcome (always 6 bleed at base dex on a fresh target) is reasonable; the defect is that the description implies a conditional ramp that never gates.
verdict.corrected_detail: Vein Splitter (t3-agility-attack-counter) description: "Deal 4([dex]) three times. Each hit applies 1[bleed]([dex]). If enemy has [bleed]: each hit applies 1 more [bleed]([dex])." The effects[] place the unconditional per-hit bleed (idx 1) BEFORE the gated per-hit bleed (idx 2, condition enemy_has_stack:'bleed'). The resolver applies effects in array order (CardResolver.ts:139-147) and the dot resolver mutates enemy bleed immediately (line 544: state.bleedStacks += appliedValue). The gate is checked against live state (line 208 readStackCount + line 210 early-return), and enemy_has_stack conditions are NOT read from the pre-consume snapshot (snapshot at line 132 is only consumed by consume_stack_value, lines 265-271). So the unconditional block sets bleed > 0 first, guaranteeing the gate passes — even on a fresh 0-bleed enemy. The "If enemy has [bleed]" clause never fails on the card's own cast; it is effectively unconditional (always +1 bleed per hit -> always 6 bleed at base dex). Fix: either reword to the always-true reality ("Each hit applies 2[bleed]([dex])") or gate the bonus on the PRE-cast bleed count by reading from the pre-consume snapshot instead of live state, so the bonus genuinely requires the enemy to already be bleeding.

finding.title: Self-cooldown penalty ("delays 4 more seconds next time") never fires — overload_lockout_ms is unwired
finding.detail: Description: "Deal 12([str]) Pierce. If you have 5 or more [rage]: deal 20([str]) more Pierce and this card delays 4 more seconds next time." The drawback is authored as overload_lockout_ms:4000 on the bonus damage effect. But CardResolver only ever surfaces extra cooldown via result.cooldownDebtSec, and that is set ONLY by the 'cd_debt' effect TYPE (CardResolver.ts:786-790). overload_lockout_ms is read nowhere in the engine — only in CardText.ts (prose) and the type def. CombatEngine.ts:347-349 consumes result.cooldownDebtSec, which this card never produces. Sim confirms cdDebt=0 even with rage>=5 and the bonus firing (42 damage). So the stated self-penalty is purely cosmetic and the card is strictly stronger than its text implies.
finding.evidence: Grep: overload_lockout_ms appears only in types.ts:168, cards.json:2402, CardText.ts:861 (cosmetic). CombatEngine reads only result.cooldownDebtSec (CombatEngine.ts:347-349); resolver sets cooldownDebtSec exclusively in the 'cd_debt' case (CardResolver.ts:789). Sim rage5: dmg=42, cooldownDebtSec=0.
finding.suggested_fix: Wire overload_lockout_ms in CardResolver (add it to result.cooldownDebtSec when present, gated by the same condition firing) OR convert the drawback into a real 'cd_debt' effect on the card. Otherwise remove the '...delays 4 more seconds next time' clause from the description.

verdict.title: Self-cooldown penalty ("delays 4 more seconds next time") never fires — overload_lockout_ms is unwired
verdict.reason: Confirmed real dead_effect. The bonus damage effect carries overload_lockout_ms:4000 (cards.json:2402), and the description (cards.json:2366) states "this card delays 4 more seconds next time." But overload_lockout_ms is read NOWHERE in the combat engine. A Grep of CardResolver.ts for overload_lockout/cooldownDebtSec/lockout returns only the ResolveResult.cooldownDebtSec field decl (line 28) and the cd_debt effect TYPE case (line 789); overload_lockout_ms never appears. The only producer of result.cooldownDebtSec is the 'cd_debt' effect type (CardResolver.ts:786-790, resolvedValue), and this card has NO cd_debt effect — only two 'damage' effects. CombatEngine.ts:347-349 stores result.cooldownDebtSec onto cdDebtBySlot, but this card never sets it, so no extra cooldown is ever applied. overload_lockout_ms otherwise exists only in types.ts:168 (type def) and CardText.ts:861-862 (cosmetic prose). The penalty is purely cosmetic and the card is strictly stronger than its text claims. Not a C1-C5 intended choice — it is an unrealized drawback. Severity med (not high) because it makes the card overperform rather than crash, and it overlaps with a misleading-description issue.
verdict.corrected_detail: Wire overload_lockout_ms in CardResolver by adding it to result.cooldownDebtSec (converted to seconds) when present and the host effect's condition fires, OR replace the drawback with a real cd_debt effect on the card. Otherwise drop the "...delays 4 more seconds next time" clause from the description. Relevant files: src/data/json/cards.json:2363-2410 (card), src/systems/combat/CardResolver.ts:786-790 (cd_debt is the only cooldownDebtSec producer), src/systems/combat/CombatEngine.ts:347-349 (consumes cooldownDebtSec), src/systems/cards/CardText.ts:861-862 (cosmetic prose for overload_lockout_ms).

finding.title: Self-armor gate (>=32) cannot be met by the card's own 26 armor at base VIT — damage half is dead without prior armor/VIT
finding.detail: Description: "Gain 26[armor]([vit]). If [armor] is at least 32: Deal 22 Pierce to all enemies." The armor effect resolves first; the damage gate self_armor_atleast:32 then reads the post-gain armor. Base grant is 26 (scale vit per:2 value:4 -> +4 only at VIT>=2, +8 at VIT>=4). From 0 armor you need floor(vit/2)*4 >= 6, i.e. VIT >= 4, OR >=6 pre-existing armor, for the 32 gate to clear. Sim: VIT1/start0 -> armor 26, dmg=0 (gate fails); VIT4/start0 -> armor 34, dmg=22; VIT1/start10 -> armor 36, dmg=22. A player reading 'Gain 26 armor. If armor >=32...' has no cue that the card's own 26 falls short of its own 32 threshold at baseline, so the offensive half silently does nothing on a clean first cast.
finding.evidence: armor effect (value 26, scale vit/2*4) precedes the damage effect; CardResolver.ts:233-235 checks self_armor_atleast against live heroDefense (read after the gain). Sim VIT1 from 0 armor: dmg=0; VIT4 from 0: dmg=22.
finding.suggested_fix: Lower the gate to <=26 (so the card's own armor satisfies it at base VIT) or raise the base armor grant to >=32, or reword the gate to make the 'needs extra armor/VIT' requirement explicit. Contrast Mountain's Will / Cliffwind Maul / Earthcleaver / Quarry Dance / Cinderquake whose armor gates read PRE-EXISTING armor and are independent of their own effects.

verdict.title: Self-armor gate (>=32) is not met by the card's own 26 armor at base VIT, so the Pierce half silently does nothing on a clean cast
verdict.reason: Confirmed by exact code/data. cards.json:2864-2883 — effect[0] armor value 26 with scale {stat:vit, per:2, value:4}; effect[1] damage value 22 pierce_armor with condition self_armor_atleast:32. Effects resolve in array order (CardResolver.ts:139 `for (const effect of effectiveEffects)`), so the armor effect runs first and mutates state.heroDefense at line 482 (`state.heroDefense += armorAmt`). The damage effect's gate is then evaluated against the LIVE post-gain armor at CardResolver.ts:233-234 (`if (state.heroDefense < cond.self_armor_atleast) return;`). Scale math at line 373: resolvedValue = 26 + floor(vit/per)*value = 26 + floor(vit/2)*4. VIT baseline is 1 (EnemyAI.ts:280 uses `readStat(state,'vit') - 1`, i.e. 1 = neutral). At VIT1 from 0 armor: floor(1/2)*4 = 0 -> armor = 26 < 32 -> damage early-returns (0). The 32 gate first clears at VIT4 (floor(4/2)*4 = 8 -> 34) or with >=6 pre-existing armor. The stored description is faithfully generated (CardText.ts:164-167 produces "If [armor] is at least 32"), so this is not a desc_mismatch or a card_bug — the gate functions as authored and "stack armor first" is a legitimate earth-tribe payoff pattern. But the self-referential numbers (Gain 26 / gate 32) give a player no cue that the card's own 26 falls 6 short of its own threshold at baseline, so the offensive half does nothing on a clean first cast. That ambiguity about HOW MUCH armor is actually needed is a genuine unclear_desc; low severity is appropriate.
verdict.corrected_detail: Description: "Gain 26[armor]([vit]). If [armor] is at least 32: Deal 22 Pierce to all enemies." Armor resolves first (CardResolver.ts:139 array-order loop -> line 482 mutates heroDefense); the damage gate self_armor_atleast:32 reads the post-gain heroDefense (CardResolver.ts:233-234). Base grant = 26 + floor(vit/2)*4 (line 373). VIT baseline is 1 (EnemyAI.ts:280). So from 0 armor: VIT1 -> 26 (gate fails, dmg 0); VIT4 -> 34 (dmg 22); VIT1 with >=6 prior armor -> >=32 (dmg 22). The card's own 26 armor is 6 short of its own 32 gate at baseline VIT, so the Pierce half silently no-ops on a clean first cast and the text gives no cue that extra VIT/armor is required.

finding.title: "+1 damage per 4[armor] you have" counts the 22 armor the same card just granted this cast
finding.detail: Description: "Gain 22[armor]([vit]). Deal 6 Pierce, +1 damage per 4[armor] you have." The armor-gain effect is first; the damage effect uses scale source:'armor' which reads live heroDefense AFTER the gain. So even from 0 starting armor the hit already counts the just-gained 22 (22/4 = +5 -> 11 damage). Sim VIT1/start0: armor 0->22, dmg=11; VIT10/start0: armor 0->42, dmg=16. This is arguably fine but the phrase 'armor you have' is timing-ambiguous: a player may not expect the brand-new armor from the same card to be included. (Body Slam Vow is the inverse — it deals damage BEFORE the armor gain, so 'per [armor] you have' there excludes the gain.)
finding.evidence: Effect order: armor (idx0) then damage with scale.source='armor' (idx1). CardResolver.ts:369-374 reads state.heroDefense after the armor mutation. Sim: from 0 armor, dmg already includes the +5 from the 22 just gained.
finding.suggested_fix: If the intent is to count pre-cast armor, snapshot armor before the gain (use preConsume.armor). If counting post-gain armor is intended, consider wording like 'per 4[armor] (including this gain)' for clarity. Low priority — purely a wording/timing clarity issue.

verdict.title: "+1 damage per 4[armor] you have" counts the 22 armor the same card just granted this cast
verdict.reason: Confirmed as a real unclear_desc finding. The card's effects[] are: idx0 armor (value 22), idx1 damage (6 Pierce) with scale {source:'armor', per:4, value:1}. CardResolver.ts:139 iterates effects in array order against shared mutable state. The armor effect mutates state.heroDefense at CardResolver.ts:482 (state.heroDefense += armorAmt) during idx0. The damage effect at idx1 has no consume_stack_value, so its scale falls through the standard path at CardResolver.ts:369-373, which reads the LIVE state.heroDefense (post-gain) for scale.source==='armor'. So from 0 starting armor: armor 0->22, then damage = 6 + floor(22/4)*1 = 11. The just-gained 22 IS counted. A preConsume snapshot DOES capture pre-cast armor (StatusEffects snapshotStacks line 49 armor: state.heroDefense), but the standard scale path never uses it, so there is no pre-cast option in play. The description "per 4[armor] you have" gives no signal that the same card's freshly-granted armor counts. This is mechanically confirmed and genuinely ambiguous, demonstrated by the inverse-ordering precedent: Body Slam Vow (t3-attack-defense-defense, cards.json:2106-2137) uses the identical phrase "per [armor] you have" but places its damage effect at idx0 BEFORE the armor gain at idx1, so there the same wording EXCLUDES the gain. Two cards, identical phrasing, opposite timing semantics, nothing in the text distinguishing them — that is the unclear_desc. It is NOT a card_bug (the card resolves and the post-gain counting is plausibly intended) and NOT covered by C1-C5, so low severity for a wording/clarity issue is correct.
verdict.corrected_detail: Aegis of Returning Wrath gains armor (idx0) then deals damage scaled by armor (idx1, scale.source='armor'), so the damage counts the armor just gained this cast. From 0 starting armor the 6 Pierce already becomes 11 (6 + floor(22/4)). Mechanically intended-or-not, the phrase "per 4[armor] you have" is timing-ambiguous: the identically-worded Body Slam Vow deals damage BEFORE its armor gain, so the same phrase there EXCLUDES the gain. Wording-only clarity issue; suggest "per 4[armor] (including this gain)" if post-gain counting is intended.

finding.title: devour exhaust_next clause does nothing — engine requires from_deck
finding.detail: Vengeful Pyre's description promises 'Exhaust the next card in order', authored as a devour effect: {"type":"devour","value":1,"target":"self_deck","devour":{"exhaust_next":true}}. But CardResolver's devour case opens with `if (!rawEffect?.devour?.from_deck) break;`. Since this effect only sets exhaust_next (no from_deck), the case breaks immediately and mutates nothing. A project-wide grep for `exhaust_next` shows it appears ONLY in types.ts (the type def), CardText.ts (prose generation), and this one card — never in CardResolver or CombatEngine. There is no devouredSlots mark, no play-queue skip, and devour_succeeded is never set. The card still grants 3 rage and the ×2-rage aura, but the advertised 'exhaust the next card' is a no-op.
finding.evidence: cards.json L4164-4178 effect {type:devour, devour:{exhaust_next:true}}; CardResolver.ts L792-796 `case 'devour': if (!rawEffect?.devour?.from_deck) break;`; grep shows exhaust_next only in types.ts:181, CardText.ts:855, cards.json:4176 (no engine handler).
finding.suggested_fix: Either implement an exhaust_next branch in the devour case (mark the next non-self deck slot in devouredSlots / a new exhaustNextSlots set that advanceDeckPointer skips), or rewrite the card so it doesn't claim a deck-exhaust it can't deliver.

verdict.title: devour exhaust_next clause does nothing — engine requires from_deck
verdict.reason: CONFIRMED. cards.json L4171-4178: the devour effect on Vengeful Pyre sets only {devour:{exhaust_next:true}}, with NO from_deck flag. CardResolver.ts L796 opens the devour case with `if (!rawEffect?.devour?.from_deck) break;`, so for this card the case breaks immediately — nothing is added to state.devouredSlots and _devourSucceeded is never set. CombatEngine.advanceDeckPointer (L537-547) skips slots ONLY by reading state.devouredSlots, so no card is ever exhausted/skipped. A project-wide grep confirms `exhaust_next` exists only in types.ts:181 (type def), CardText.ts:853-855 (prose generation), the card data (cards.json:4176), and non-engine script/audit artifacts (apply-rebalance-reworks.mjs, sim-report*.json, chunk JSONs) — it is never read by CardResolver or CombatEngine. Therefore the stored description's clause "Exhaust the next card in order" is a genuine no-op (dead_effect + desc/engine mismatch). The card's other two effects work: it still grants 3 rage (stack effect, L4165-4170) and applies the permanent double-rage aura (L4179+), so this is a dead CLAUSE on an otherwise-functional card, not a dead card — hence severity adjusted high->med. The auditor's evidence and line references are accurate.
verdict.corrected_detail: Vengeful Pyre (t3-counter-counter-fire) authors its "Exhaust the next card in order" promise as {type:devour, target:self_deck, devour:{exhaust_next:true}} (cards.json L4171-4178). But CardResolver's devour case (L792-822) starts with `if (!rawEffect?.devour?.from_deck) break;` (L796) — since this effect sets only exhaust_next and not from_deck, the case exits before touching state.devouredSlots, and _devourSucceeded is never set. CombatEngine.advanceDeckPointer (L537-547) only skips slots present in state.devouredSlots, so nothing is ever exhausted. The exhaust_next field is read nowhere in the engine (grep: only types.ts type def, CardText.ts prose at L853-855, the card data, and offline script/audit artifacts). Net effect: the rage gain and the rest-of-combat double-rage aura both work, but the advertised deck-exhaust clause is a silent no-op. Fix: either implement an exhaust_next branch in the devour case (mark the next non-self deck slot so advanceDeckPointer skips it) or rewrite the description to drop the deck-exhaust it cannot deliver. finding.title: overload_lockout_ms field is never read by the engine — no cooldown penalty applied
finding.detail: Cleaver's Tax's Vengeance hit carries `overload_lockout_ms: 4000`, and its generated description ends '...and this card delays 4 more seconds next time' (CardText.ts L861-864 appends that clause whenever overload_lockout_ms is set). But the resolver never reads overload_lockout_ms. Cooldown debt is only produced by the `cd_debt` EFFECT TYPE (CardResolver L786-790 → result.cooldownDebtSec), consumed in CombatEngine L347-349. A grep for overload_lockout_ms in src/systems shows hits only in CardText.ts (prose), never in CardResolver/CombatEngine. So the card applies its big Vengeance Pierce with zero cooldown penalty — the drawback the description advertises does not exist.
finding.evidence: cards.json L2402 `"overload_lockout_ms": 4000` on the conditional damage effect; CardText.ts L861-864 builds the 'delays 4 more seconds' suffix; CardResolver.ts cd_debt case L786-790 (only path that sets cooldownDebtSec); grep overload_lockout_ms → only CardText.ts:861/862/1146 (no engine read).
finding.suggested_fix: Either route overload_lockout_ms through ResolveResult.cooldownDebtSec (e.g. in the damage case, add `result.cooldownDebtSec += overload_lockout_ms/1000` when the effect fires), or convert the drawback to a real `cd_debt` effect like Brine Crucible uses, so the advertised penalty is realized.

verdict.title: overload_lockout_ms field is never read by the engine — no cooldown penalty applied
verdict.reason: Confirmed by direct code/data inspection. Card t3-attack-counter-counter (Cleaver's Tax), cards.json L2363-2411, stores description (L2366): "...deal 20([str]) more Pierce and this card delays 4 more seconds next time." That suffix is generated ONLY from overload_lockout_ms:4000 on the conditional damage effect (cards.json L2402) via CardText.ts L861-864 (if (fx.overload_lockout_ms && body) { ...delays ${s} more seconds next time }). A grep for overload_lockout_ms across src/ returns hits ONLY in CardText.ts (prose, L861/862/1146) and types.ts (schema decl, L168) — never in CardResolver.ts or CombatEngine.ts. The resolver's damage case (CardResolver.ts L377-450) never references overload_lockout_ms and never sets result.cooldownDebtSec. The sole path that populates cooldownDebtSec is the dedicated cd_debt effect TYPE (CardResolver.ts L786-790: result.cooldownDebtSec = (result.cooldownDebtSec ?? 0) + Math.max(0, resolvedValue)), consumed by CombatEngine L347-349 into cdDebtBySlot. Cleaver's Tax has no cd_debt effect — its penalty is encoded as a damage-effect field the engine ignores. A real cd_debt effect type does exist and is used by another card (cards.json L6900), proving the working mechanism was available but not used here. Net: the Vengeance Pierce fires with zero cooldown penalty; the advertised drawback does not exist. This is a genuine dead_effect / desc-vs-engine mismatch, not a C1-C5 intended choice and not an auditor misread. Med severity: real and makes the card stronger than its text, but isolated to one mid-tier card.
verdict.corrected_detail: overload_lockout_ms is a vestigial/never-wired field. CardText.ts L861-864 turns it into the "delays 4 more seconds next time" clause, but no engine code reads it. The only functional cooldown-penalty path is the cd_debt effect TYPE (CardResolver L786-790 → result.cooldownDebtSec → CombatEngine L347-349 → cdDebtBySlot). Cleaver's Tax encodes its penalty as overload_lockout_ms:4000 on a damage effect (cards.json L2402) instead of a cd_debt effect, so the drawback is purely cosmetic. Fix: either route overload_lockout_ms into result.cooldownDebtSec inside the damage case when the effect actually fires (after the condition passes), or replace it with a real cd_debt effect like the card at cards.json L6900 uses.

finding.title: cd_debt prose says 'Next card delays' but the penalty hits THIS card's own slot
finding.detail: Brine Crucible's description ends 'Next card delays 3 more seconds.', generated by CardText.cdDebtBody ('Next card delays ${fx.value} more seconds'). The engine, however, stores cd_debt on the slot of the card that produced it (slot = this.deckPointer, i.e. Brine Crucible itself) and adds it to that SAME slot's next cooldown — not the following card. CombatEngine L343-349 keys cdDebtBySlot by the current slot, and L365-369 adds debtMs to the just-played card's own next cooldown. So the delay applies to Brine Crucible the next time it comes up, not to the next card played. Notably the OTHER cooldown-penalty card (Cleaver's Tax) describes the identical mechanism correctly as 'this card delays ... next time', so the wording is inconsistent and misleading to a player.
finding.evidence: cards.json L6866 description '... Next card delays 3 more seconds.'; CardText.ts L608-610 cdDebtBody returns 'Next card delays N more seconds'; CombatEngine.ts L343-349 `const slot = this.deckPointer; ... this.state.cdDebtBySlot[slot] = ... + cooldownDebtSec*1000` and L365-369 applies `+ debtMs` to that same slot's cooldown.
finding.suggested_fix: Change cdDebtBody to mirror the overload wording, e.g. 'This card delays N more seconds next time', so the generated/stored text matches the per-slot debt the engine actually applies.

verdict.title: cd_debt prose says 'Next card delays' but the penalty hits THIS card's own slot
verdict.reason: Confirmed by direct code reading. cards.json L6866 stores "...Next card delays 3 more seconds." and the effect is {type:"cd_debt", value:3, target:"self"} (L6900-6903). That text is generated by CardText.ts cdDebtBody (L608-610: `Next card delays ${fx.value} more seconds`). But CombatEngine.ts keys the debt to the PRODUCING card's own slot, not the next card: L343 `const slot = this.deckPointer` (the slot of the card being resolved = Brine Crucible itself), L347-349 store `cdDebtBySlot[slot] += cooldownDebtSec*1000`, and L344-345/365-369 read+clear `cdDebtBySlot[slot]` and add `debtMs` to that SAME slot's next cooldown. Since the engine plays the deck in fixed order, the +3s delay applies to Brine Crucible the next time its own slot comes up, not to whatever card is played next. The OTHER cd_debt card, Cleaver's Tax (cards.json L2366), describes the identical per-slot mechanism correctly as "this card delays 4 more seconds next time", proving the wording is inconsistent and misleading. This is a genuine desc_mismatch / unclear_desc, not an intended-by-design (C1-C5) choice. Severity stays low: purely a text-clarity issue, no mechanical breakage. Suggested fix: change cdDebtBody to mirror Cleaver's Tax, e.g. "This card delays N more seconds next time".
verdict.corrected_detail: Stored description "Next card delays 3 more seconds." (cards.json L6866) is wrong about WHICH card is delayed. The cd_debt effect (target:"self") is keyed in CombatEngine to the producing card's own deck slot (slot = this.deckPointer at L343), so the +3s is added to Brine Crucible's OWN next cooldown (L344-349, L365-369), not to the card played immediately after. With fixed-order deck play this means the delay lands on Brine Crucible the next cycle. CardText.cdDebtBody (L608-610) generates the misleading wording; Cleaver's Tax (L2366) describes the same engine mechanism accurately ("this card delays ... next time").

finding.title: green_field terrain references 3 phantom enemies that do not exist in enemies.json
finding.detail: terrain-enemies.json defines a `green_field` pool: base ['slime'], addAtLoop {5:['red_slime'], 10:['earth_dragon']}. None of slime/red_slime/earth_dragon exist in enemies.json (26-enemy roster confirmed; cross-check shows exactly these 3 missing). green_field is also NOT a placeable terrain: src/data/tiles.json has only basic/forest/graveyard/swamp/desert/lava, and TileRegistry.TerrainType = 'forest'|'graveyard'|'swamp'|'desert'|'lava' (no green_field). So the pool is currently unreachable dead data rather than a live crash. But it is a latent landmine: if green_field ever became placeable, getEnemyPoolForTerrain('green_field') returns ['slime',...], the tile gets enemyId='slime', and CombatScene.create() -> getEnemyById('slime') returns undefined and silently bails (no combat, no crash, but a world-map tile with an unspawnable enemy). Preloader.ts (lines 104-106) and GameScene still load slime/red_slime/earth_dragon sprites and the bg_green_field background, reinforcing that this is half-removed content.
finding.evidence: terrain-enemies.json lines 80-92: "green_field": { "base": ["slime"], "addAtLoop": { "5": ["red_slime"], "10": ["earth_dragon"] } }. enemies.json contains none of these 3 ids. src/data/tiles.json terrains: forest/graveyard/swamp/desert/lava only. LootGenerator.getEnemyPoolForTerrain returns the raw pool with no existence check; CombatScene line 283-288 returns early when getEnemyById is undefined.
finding.suggested_fix: Delete the green_field entry from terrain-enemies.json (and the orphaned slime/red_slime/earth_dragon Preloader/GameScene assets) since the terrain is not placeable; OR add the 3 enemies to enemies.json and register green_field as a placeable terrain. Either way, harden getEnemyPoolForTerrain / assignEnemies to drop ids not present in enemies.json so no tile is ever assigned an unspawnable enemyId.

verdict.title: green_field terrain pool references 3 enemy ids that do not exist in enemies.json (orphaned dead data + assets)

finding.title: Terrain tiles carry combatChance:1.0 but spawn chance is hardcoded to 0.5 and never reads the field
finding.detail: Every terrain tile in src/data/tiles.json sets "combatChance": 1.0, and TileConfig declares `combatChance?: number`. But nothing reads it: a project-wide search for `combatChance` finds only the JSON data and the type declaration. The real terrain spawn probability is the hardcoded LoopRunner.COMBAT_TILE_SPAWN_CHANCE = 0.5 (used in assignEnemies for terrain/subtile tiles via getCombatSpawnChance). So the authored 1.0 is dead data. The user-facing tile descriptions ('50% chance to spawn a <terrain> enemy') correctly match the real 0.5 behavior — meaning the data field (1.0) contradicts both the description and the engine.
finding.evidence: tiles.json line 3: "forest": { ... "combatChance": 1.0 } with description "...50% chance to spawn a forest enemy.". LoopRunner.ts line 57: COMBAT_TILE_SPAWN_CHANCE = 0.5; getCombatSpawnChance() returns that base (+War Horn). grep for combatChance: only tiles.json data + TileRegistry.ts:26 type decl, zero readers.
finding.suggested_fix: Either remove the unused combatChance field from tiles.json + TileConfig, or make getCombatSpawnChance read the tile's combatChance as its base instead of the hardcoded 0.5. If kept as dead data, at minimum set it to 0.5 so it stops contradicting the description.

verdict.title: Terrain tiles carry combatChance:1.0 but spawn chance is hardcoded to 0.5 and never reads the field
verdict.reason: Confirmed real data_inconsistency. (1) src/data/tiles.json lines 3-7: all five terrain tiles (forest/graveyard/swamp/desert/lava) carry "combatChance": 1.0, each with a description stating "50% chance to spawn a <terrain> enemy." (2) Project-wide grep for "combatChance" returns ONLY the JSON data (tiles.json:3-7) and the type declaration TileRegistry.ts:26 ("combatChance?: number") — zero readers anywhere. (3) The real spawn probability is hardcoded: LoopRunner.ts:57 declares COMBAT_TILE_SPAWN_CHANCE = 0.5. In assignEnemies the 'terrain' case (LoopRunner.ts:349-350) calls getCombatSpawnChance(i), which (LoopRunner.ts:399-404) initializes `chance = COMBAT_TILE_SPAWN_CHANCE` (0.5) and only adds War Horn boost — it never reads the tile's combatChance field. So the authored 1.0 is dead data that contradicts both the engine's real 0.5 base and the user-facing 50% descriptions. The active file is src/data/tiles.json (imported by TileRegistry.ts:1); the unrelated src/data/json/tiles.json is a tile-type list with no terrain/combatChance entries, so the finding targeted the correct file. No gameplay impact (field is unused), hence low severity is correct.
verdict.corrected_detail: The combatChance field on all 5 terrain tiles in src/data/tiles.json is dead/unused data. The engine ignores it entirely: terrain spawn rolls use the hardcoded LoopRunner.COMBAT_TILE_SPAWN_CHANCE = 0.5 (LoopRunner.ts:57, read in getCombatSpawnChance at lines 399-404). The 1.0 value contradicts both the engine (0.5) and the tile descriptions ("50% chance"). Fix: either delete combatChance from tiles.json + TileConfig, or make getCombatSpawnChance use the tile's combatChance as its base; if kept, set it to 0.5 to stop contradicting the description.

finding.title: The 'should-be-easy' first boss has an offensive affinity that raises its effective per-hit damage ~67%
finding.detail: Design intent (and the on-disk boss-progression sim) says doom_knight is meant to be the easy first boss, yet it carries affinity:'counter'. EnemyAffinity 'counter' applies applyHeroDamage(2*m) on every landed attack, with m=2 for bosses => 4 extra hero HP loss per attack (routed through armor, skipRelics=true). doom_knight base damage is 6, so each attack effectively costs the hero ~10 HP (6 + 4 counter), a ~67% uplift, on top of the enrage x1.4 below 50% HP. This is a static contributor to the already-documented BP-1 finding that doom_knight is not easy. (Counter affinity is fine in principle; the smell is pairing the most aggressive damage-amplifying affinity with the boss that is explicitly supposed to be the gentle introduction.)
finding.evidence: enemies.json doom_knight: damage 6, affinity 'counter', behaviors enrage hpThreshold 0.5 x1.4. EnemyAffinity.ts counter branch: const bleed = 2 * m; applyHeroDamage(bleed, ...) with m = isBoss?2:1 => 4/attack. boss-progression/findings.json BP-1 already shows doom_knight is not winnable comfortably for average loop-10 decks.
finding.suggested_fix: Give doom_knight a non-damage or defensive affinity (e.g. 'attack' = no secondary, or 'defense') so the intro boss does not silently add 4 HP/hit, OR cut its base damage to absorb the counter uplift (the sim's BP-1 already proposes 6->… damage tuning). Keep counter for a later boss where extra pressure is intended.

verdict.title: Intended-easy first boss carries 'counter' affinity, adding +4 HP/hit (~67% per-hit uplift over base damage 6)
verdict.reason: Mechanical facts confirmed against current source. enemies.json doom_knight (lines 367-399): type "boss", attack.damage 6, affinity "counter", enrage hpThreshold 0.5 attackSpeedMultiplier 1.4. EnemyAffinity.ts counter branch (lines 60-67): const bleed = 2 * m; applyHeroDamage(bleed, state, /*skipRelics=*/true) with m = isBoss ? 2 : 1 (line 29) => 4 HP per attack for a boss, routed through armor (skipRelics true so iron_will/phoenix already-fired relics aren't re-triggered). EnemyAI.ts calls applyEnemyAffinityEffect once per attack at lines 159 (multi-hit path) and 196 (single-hit path), passing state.enemyType === 'boss', so the +4 fires on every landed attack. Base 6 + counter 4 = ~10 effective pre-armor HP/hit, the ~67% uplift the finding cites, on top of the enrage x1.4 below 50% HP. The "easy first boss" intent is corroborated by tests/audit/wf/boss-progression/findings.json, whose summary explicitly calls doom_knight "the should-be-easy first boss" and BP-1 (severity high, confidence high) shows it is not comfortably winnable for average loop-10 decks (only 6/8 win, 2/8 with >=15% cushion, 2 archetypes lose outright). This is a legitimate balance_smell: counter affinity is not forbidden by C1-C5 (those locks govern card mechanics, not which affinity an enemy carries), so it is not a card_bug or enemy_bug, but pairing the most damage-amplifying affinity with the intended gentle intro boss is a defensible numeric outlier. Severity kept at med per the instruction to keep balance_smells light. One correction to the auditor's evidence: it quoted BP-1's "9dmg" figure; the CURRENT on-disk attack.damage is 6 (BP-1 was sim'd against an older 9dmg config). The core claim (base 6, counter +4, ~67% uplift) matches the current data; the dependency on the 9dmg sim should be read as directional, not exact.
verdict.corrected_detail: Design intent (and tests/audit/wf/boss-progression/findings.json) treats doom_knight as the easy first boss, yet it carries affinity 'counter'. EnemyAffinity.ts (lines 60-67) applies applyHeroDamage(2*m) on every landed attack with m=2 for bosses => +4 hero HP loss per attack (routed through armor, skipRelics=true). doom_knight's current base damage is 6 (enemies.json line 374; note the auditor cited BP-1's stale 9dmg figure), so each attack effectively costs ~10 HP (6 + 4 counter) pre-armor, a ~67% uplift, on top of enrage x1.4 below 50% HP. The affinity fires once per attack via EnemyAI.ts lines 159/196. Counter affinity is mechanically fine and not C1-C5-restricted; the smell is pairing the most aggressive damage-amplifying affinity with the boss explicitly meant as the gentle introduction. Suggested direction: give doom_knight 'attack' (no secondary) or a defensive affinity, or reduce base damage to absorb the counter uplift; keep counter for a later boss where extra pressure is intended.

finding.target: desert_golem & boss_iron_golem (shield behavior, uncapped enemy armor)
finding.title: Boss 'shield' armor gain is uncapped while affinity armor is capped, hard-countering direct-damage decks in long fights
finding.detail: EnemyAI.applyPeriodicBehaviors adds shield armor with `state.enemyDefense += shield.shieldAmount` and NO cap, on a fixed interval. desert_golem = +10 every 7s, boss_iron_golem = +12 every 7s (the latter also has defense-affinity +6/attack, capped 60). CardResolver subtracts effectiveEnemyDef from EVERY direct-damage hit (perHit = max(1, floor(baseDmg - effectiveEnemyDef))), so as armor ratchets, a direct-damage deck is floored to 1 dmg/hit. Simulating boss_iron_golem over 60s: shield adds 96 (uncapped) + affinity 60 (capped) = +156 effective defense. Meanwhile DoTs (poison/bleed/burn) apply `state.enemyHP -= dmg` with NO enemyDefense subtraction (CombatEngine tickActiveDoTs), so DoT decks ignore the armor entirely. Result: the two shield-bosses asymmetrically wall direct-damage builds the longer the fight runs, while DoT builds are unaffected — a control/race-axis distortion not present on the other bosses. EnemyAffinity already caps defense/earth armor (25/60 and 15/40); the shield behavior should similarly cap.
finding.evidence: EnemyAI.ts lines 85-93: state.enemyDefense += shield.shieldAmount (no Math.min cap). enemies.json desert_golem shield {interval:7000, shieldAmount:10}, boss_iron_golem shield {interval:7000, shieldAmount:12}. CardResolver.ts line 442: Math.max(1, Math.floor(baseDmg - effectiveEnemyDef)) per hit; line 437 effectiveEnemyDef = enemyDefense + def auras. CombatEngine.ts lines 434/462/485: DoT ticks do `state.enemyHP -= dmg` with no defense subtraction. 60s sim: +156 effective defense for boss_iron_golem.
finding.suggested_fix: Cap cumulative shield-derived enemy defense (e.g. clamp state.enemyDefense to a per-boss ceiling in the shield branch, mirroring the affinity caps of 40/60), so direct-damage decks aren't floored to 1/hit in long fights. Optionally let very high enemy armor decay or give a small armor-pierce baseline. This evens the direct-vs-DoT matchup spread on the two tank bosses.

verdict.target: desert_golem & boss_iron_golem (uncapped shield armor vs. capped affinity armor)
verdict.title: Boss 'shield' armor gain is uncapped while affinity armor is capped, asymmetrically walling direct-damage decks in long fights
verdict.reason: The core mechanic is REAL and verified against the code. EnemyAI.ts:88-91 does `state.enemyDefense += shield.shieldAmount` with NO Math.min cap, on a fixed 7000ms interval (desert_golem +10, boss_iron_golem +12; confirmed in enemies.json lines 653-659 and 726-737). CardResolver.ts:439-442 floors every direct hit: `Math.max(1, Math.floor(baseDmg - effectiveEnemyDef))`, so as enemyDefense ratchets, direct-damage hits collapse toward 1/hit. Meanwhile CombatEngine.tickActiveDoTs does `state.enemyHP -= dmg` (poison line 434, bleed line 462, burn line 485) with NO defense subtraction, so DoT decks bypass the ratchet entirely. EnemyAffinity.ts DOES cap its armor (defense 60/25 at lines 41-42, earth 40/15 at line 102), confirming the inconsistency the auditor flagged: affinity armor is bounded, shield armor is not. So the direction of the claim (uncapped shield asymmetrically walls direct-damage builds while DoTs ignore it) is confirmed. HOWEVER the auditor's quantification is WRONG: shield and affinity write the SAME `state.enemyDefense` variable, so they do not simply sum to +156. On boss_iron_golem (affinity 'defense', cap 60), once shield pushes enemyDefense above 60, the affinity branch `next = Math.min(60, enemyDefense+gain)` returns 60, which is BELOW current, so a defense-affinity attack actually clamps enemyDefense back DOWN to 60 (EnemyAffinity.ts:42-44) — partially self-limiting on that boss. desert_golem's 'earth' affinity (line 104) only adds when `enemyDefense < cap`, so it stops contributing but does NOT pull shield down. Net: the wall is real but the +156 figure is overstated/incorrect. Severity lowered to low because (a) the kind is balance_smell and the brief says sim-based balance is handled elsewhere / keep light, (b) armor flooring direct dmg and DoTs bypassing armor are intended C-design choices (poison/bleed/burn quadratic & bank-and-cash, no defense read), so the only genuinely actionable item is the uncapped-vs-capped inconsistency on these two tank bosses — not a correctness bug.
verdict.corrected_detail: Real mechanic, corrected math. EnemyAI.applyPeriodicBehaviors (EnemyAI.ts:88-91) adds shield armor uncapped: `state.enemyDefense += shield.shieldAmount` every 7000ms (desert_golem +10, boss_iron_golem +12). Direct damage is floored by it (CardResolver.ts:442: `Math.max(1, Math.floor(baseDmg - effectiveEnemyDef))`), while DoTs bypass it (CombatEngine.ts:434/462/485 subtract dmg from HP with no defense term) — so the longer the fight runs, the harder these two tank bosses wall direct-damage builds while leaving DoT builds untouched. This contrasts with EnemyAffinity.ts which DOES cap armor (defense 60, earth 40 for bosses). NOTE the prior auditor's '+156 effective defense' is incorrect: shield and affinity share the same enemyDefense variable. On boss_iron_golem (affinity 'defense', cap 60) a defense-affinity attack uses `Math.min(60, enemyDefense+6)`, which once shield has pushed defense past 60 actually clamps it back DOWN to 60 (EnemyAffinity.ts:42-44), so the two contributions don't sum — boss_iron_golem is partly self-limiting via its own affinity. desert_golem ('earth', adds only while `enemyDefense < 40`, EnemyAffinity.ts:104) lets shield ratchet unbounded since earth never pulls it down. The genuine issue is the uncapped-shield-vs-capped-affinity inconsistency, not the magnitude claimed. Suggested fix unchanged in spirit: clamp the shield branch to a per-boss ceiling mirroring the affinity caps, or give a baseline armor-pierce.

finding.title: Agility affinity speedup is almost fully cancelled by the cooldown-recovery rate (≈1 extra attack per 30s)
finding.detail: The 'agility' affinity is the speed identity for pocket_cat/vampire/werewolf/scorpion. On each attack it shaves enemyAttackCooldown by 100*m (100 for a normal enemy), floored at 800. But EnemyAI.tick recovers enemyAttackCooldown toward base at 0.1*deltaMs = 100ms recovered per 1000ms of real time. Since these enemies attack roughly every ~2.2s, recovery (~220ms between attacks) outpaces the per-attack shave (100ms) ~2:1, so the cooldown settles ~80ms below base and never compounds. Simulated over 30s a normal agility enemy lands ~14 attacks vs ~13 without the affinity — a near-negligible payoff for what is sold as the speed archetype. Not broken (it does net a hair faster), but the 'speed identity' is effectively cosmetic.
finding.evidence: EnemyAffinity.ts lines 48-58: shave = 100 * m; enemyAttackCooldown = max(800, before - shave). EnemyAI.ts lines 45-51: recover = deltaMs * 0.1; enemyAttackCooldown = min(base, +recover). 30s sim (base cd 2215, shave 100/atk, recover 0.1*dt): 14 attacks with affinity vs 13 without; steady-state cd ~2135 (only ~80ms below base).
finding.suggested_fix: Either lower the recovery rate (e.g. 0.03-0.05*deltaMs) or raise the per-attack shave / make the shave a persistent stacking buff up to a floor, so agility enemies are perceptibly faster. Alternatively make agility a flat % cooldown reduction applied to enemyBaseAttackCooldown so it doesn't fight its own recovery loop.

verdict.title: Agility affinity speedup is almost fully cancelled by the cooldown-recovery rate (~0-1 extra attacks per 30s)
verdict.reason: Confirmed against the code and data. EnemyAffinity.ts:53-55 shaves enemyAttackCooldown by 100ms (m=1 for normal enemies), floored at 800: `state.enemyAttackCooldown = Math.max(800, before - shave)`. EnemyAI.ts:45-50 recovers it back toward enemyBaseAttackCooldown at `deltaMs * 0.1` = 100ms recovered per 1000ms of real time, and EnemyAI.ts:55/67-68 refills cooldownTimer from that (shaved) enemyAttackCooldown. The four agility enemies in enemies.json have base attackCooldown 2215 (pocket_cat), 2100 (vampire), 2250 (werewolf), 2400 (scorpion). Per attack cycle (~2.1-2.4s) the recovery adds back ~210-240ms toward base while each attack only shaves 100ms, so recovery outpaces the shave ~2:1 and enemyAttackCooldown ratchets straight back to base (hitting the cap), never accumulating. My step simulation over 30s (dt=16ms and dt=100ms): pocket_cat 14 attacks with affinity vs 13 without (+1), vampire 14 vs 14 (+0), werewolf 13 vs 13 (+0), scorpion 13 vs 12 (+1); steady-state cd settles only ~80-100ms below base. The auditor's numbers and reasoning are accurate. It is NOT a misread and NOT intended-by-design — there's no C1-C5 directive that says the speed identity should be cosmetic. Keeping severity low because the effect is correctly wired and does net a hair faster (it doesn't error or fire on the wrong target); the problem is purely that the magnitude is swamped by the self-recovery loop, so the 'speed archetype' is perceptually invisible.
verdict.corrected_detail: The 'agility' affinity is the speed identity for pocket_cat/vampire/werewolf/scorpion. On each landed attack EnemyAffinity.ts shaves enemyAttackCooldown by 100ms (floored at 800), and that shaved value is what refills the next countdown (EnemyAI.getEffectiveCooldown reads state.enemyAttackCooldown). But EnemyAI.tick recovers enemyAttackCooldown toward enemyBaseAttackCooldown at 0.1*deltaMs (100ms per 1000ms real time). For these enemies (base 2100-2400ms) each attack cycle restores ~210-240ms toward base versus the 100ms per-attack shave, so recovery outpaces the shave ~2:1; enemyAttackCooldown ratchets back to base and the shave never compounds, settling only ~80-100ms below base. Over 30s a normal agility enemy lands 0-1 extra attacks vs no affinity (pocket_cat 14 vs 13; vampire/werewolf +0; scorpion 13 vs 12). Not broken — it nets a hair faster — but the 'speed identity' payoff is effectively negligible/cosmetic. Suggested fix: lower the recovery rate (e.g. 0.03-0.05*deltaMs), raise/stack the per-attack shave, or model agility as a flat % reduction on enemyBaseAttackCooldown so it doesn't fight its own recovery loop.

finding.title: Non-'fixed' attack patterns and all enemySpecialEffect branches are unreachable — no enemy data uses them
finding.detail: EnemyAI.calculateDamage implements four patterns (fixed/random/scaling/conditional) and EnemyAI.attack implements four specialEffect branches (double/stun/debuff/lifesteal). But all 26 enemies in enemies.json use "pattern": "fixed" (26/26) and NONE define attack.specialEffect. So the random/scaling/conditional damage formulas and the double/stun/debuff/lifesteal handlers (incl. the single-hit lifesteal/debuff post-processing and the 'scaling' cardsPlayed*0.16 growth) are dead branches in live play. Drain heal also has a single-hit fallback that only fires on a drain boss without multi_hit — but both drain bosses (bog_witch) also carry multi_hit, so the single-hit drain path (EnemyAI lines 186-190) is likewise never exercised.
finding.evidence: enemies.json: 26 occurrences of "pattern", all 'fixed'; zero specialEffect fields. EnemyAI.ts calculateDamage cases random/scaling/conditional (lines 209-217) and attack() specialEffect switch (lines 104-120) + single-hit lifesteal/debuff/drain (lines 174-190). bog_witch is the only 'drain' boss and it also has multi_hit, so the single-hit drain branch (lines 186-190) never runs.
finding.suggested_fix: No action required for correctness — but either author at least one enemy that uses a non-fixed pattern/specialEffect to justify the code, or trim the unused branches to reduce maintenance surface. Document that 'fixed' is the only live pattern.

verdict.target: EnemyAI attack patterns ('random'/'scaling'/'conditional') and specialEffects ('double'/'stun'/'debuff'/'lifesteal'), plus single-hit drain fallback
verdict.title: Non-'fixed' attack patterns and all enemySpecialEffect branches are unreachable — no enemy data uses them
verdict.reason: Confirmed dead code. (1) enemies.json has 26/26 enemies with \"pattern\": \"fixed\" and ZERO specialEffect fields (grep verified). (2) state.enemyPattern and state.enemySpecialEffect are populated ONLY from CombatState.ts:256-257 (enemy.attack.pattern / enemy.attack.specialEffect ?? null) with no runtime mutation anywhere in src. Therefore enemyPattern is always 'fixed' and enemySpecialEffect is always null. (3) Consequences: EnemyAI.calculateDamage cases 'random'/'scaling'/'conditional' (lines 209-217) never execute; the specialEffect switch 'double'/'stun' (lines 107-120), both 'debuff' blocks (lines 152-153, 174-175), and the 'lifesteal' block (lines 178-183) never fire. (4) Single-hit drain branch (lines 186-190) is also dead: the only 'drain' enemy is bog_witch (exactly 1 \"type\": \"drain\" in the file, line 617), and its behaviors array also contains multi_hit (line 621), so the multi-hit path handles drain at lines 145-149 and returns at line 166 before single-hit drain can run. This is a maintenance/dead-code observation, not a C1-C5 design lever and not a misread of the resolver, so it is a real (low-severity) dead_effect finding.
verdict.corrected_detail: All four non-'fixed' damage-pattern formulas and all four enemySpecialEffect handlers are unreachable in live play. enemies.json defines 26 enemies, all with attack.pattern == 'fixed' and none with attack.specialEffect. Since CombatState.ts:256-257 is the sole writer of state.enemyPattern/enemySpecialEffect (no later mutation), enemyPattern is permanently 'fixed' and enemySpecialEffect permanently null. Dead branches: EnemyAI.ts calculateDamage 'random'/'scaling'/'conditional' (209-217); attack() 'double'/'stun' switch (107-120); 'debuff' post-damage (152-153 and 174-175); 'lifesteal' (178-183). The single-hit drain fallback (186-190) is also dead because the only drain enemy, bog_witch (line 617), also carries multi_hit (line 621); the multi-hit path applies drain (145-149) and returns (166) before the single-hit path is reached. No correctness bug — drain still works via the multi-hit path; this is purely unused code surface. Fix is optional: either author an enemy using a non-fixed pattern/specialEffect, or trim the unused branches and document that 'fixed' is the only live pattern.

finding.title: iron_golem's shield (+4/8s) is a sharp outlier below the other two shield tanks (+10/7s, +12/7s)
finding.detail: Among the three 'shield' tank bosses, iron_golem grants only +4 armor every 8000ms, whereas desert_golem grants +10/7000ms and boss_iron_golem +12/7000ms — a 2.5x–3x gap in per-tick armor plus a slower interval. iron_golem is the earlier boss so a weaker shield is defensible as progression, but the documented BP-4 sim finding is the opposite: iron_golem is actually the *hardest* early boss for race decks (def 4 + 409 HP push the kill past the enrage window). So the weak shield isn't what makes it hard; its baseDefense 4 + HP do. Flagging the shield value as a numeric outlier worth a deliberate look during the boss-curve smoothing pass (BP-4), since 'shield +4' may be a leftover/under-tuned value rather than intentional.
finding.evidence: enemies.json: iron_golem shield {interval:8000, shieldAmount:4}; desert_golem {7000,10}; boss_iron_golem {7000,12}. boss-progression/findings.json BP-4 flags iron_golem (def 4, 409 HP) as harder than the two bosses after it.
finding.suggested_fix: During the BP-4 curve-smoothing pass, decide whether iron_golem's shield should be raised toward the tank peers (e.g. +6-8) or whether its baseDefense 4 should drop to 3 (BP-4's proposal). Pick one lever deliberately so the +4 shield isn't an accidental under-tune.

verdict.title: iron_golem's shield (+4/8s) is a sharp outlier below the other two shield tanks (+10/7s, +12/7s)
verdict.reason: All evidence verified against source. enemies.json: iron_golem behaviors[0] = {type:"shield", interval:8000, shieldAmount:4} (lines 414-417); desert_golem = {7000, 10} (lines 654-658); boss_iron_golem = {7000, 12} (lines 728-731). All three are bossType:"tank". So the claimed 2.5x-3x per-tick armor gap plus the slower 8000ms-vs-7000ms interval are exactly correct. The shield IS functional and not a bug: EnemyAI.ts:90 does state.enemyDefense += shield.shieldAmount once per interval, so +4 is a real (just low) value, correctly framed as a balance_smell rather than a card_bug/dead_effect. The BP-4 cross-reference is also accurate: wf/boss-progression/findings.json BP-4 (lines 40-47, severity med, confidence high) confirms iron_golem (def 4, 409 scaled HP) is harder than the two bosses after it (lizard_king def 3, bog_witch def 2) for race decks, and attributes the difficulty to baseDefense 4 + HP, NOT the shield. boss-prog-final.json confirms enemyScaledHP 409. The finding is honest and self-limiting: it states the weak shield is NOT the difficulty driver, that a weaker shield on the earlier boss is defensible progression, and only flags +4 as a numeric outlier worth a deliberate look during the BP-4 curve-smoothing pass. This is a legitimate light balance observation, not a misread and not a false-flag of an intended C1-C5 choice (no design rule sets shield values). Severity stays low as authored.
verdict.corrected_detail: Confirmed as a low-severity numeric outlier, not a bug. The shield resolver (EnemyAI.ts:90) works correctly, so +4/8000ms is a functional but under-curve value relative to its two tank peers (+10/7000ms, +12/7000ms). The finding correctly decouples this from iron_golem's actual difficulty driver (baseDefense 4 + 409 HP per BP-4), so the recommended action is the same as BP-4: pick one lever deliberately during the curve-smoothing pass — either raise the shield toward peers (~+6-8) or drop baseDefense 4->3 — rather than leaving +4 as an accidental under-tune.
